EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second major severe weather outbreak for the month occurred on the 26th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a shortwave trough centered in the middle of the country with a 60 kt mid-level jet moved across northern Arkansas. A weak surface low was moving into SE Missouri during the morning with a secondary low located further northwest. A warm frontal boundary was draped across our area. A bowing line progressed east across SE Missouri into W Kentucky and far S Illinois between 0500-1000CST, followed by a mostly sub-severe second line that exited the region by 1300CST. The environment recovered producing fuel for more severe storms, starting with supercells 1800-2100CST with a line of storms moving east-southeastward across the entire Quad State after 1900CST, crossing through by midnight. Across the Quad State, this event produced the record for most tornado track miles in a single day in office history, along with the second most warnings issued in a day (just behind the May 8, 2024 event). Widespread straight-line wind and tornado damage resulted in over 100,000 customers without power across the Quad State.||For the morning, MLCAPE values at 0700CST were 1000-2000 J/kg in W Kentucky. Effective bulk shear was 30-40 kts with STP around 1, which increased in the following hours. Precipitable water was 1.5-1.8 inches, allowing for torrential rainfall rates.||The morning round of storms produced widespread destructive straight-line wind damage leading to numerous power outages across the southernmost portions of W Kentucky from the Jackson Purchase Area to the Southern Pennyrile. Two EF-1 tornadoes occurred in Calloway County, with one of them tracking 22 miles. One person was injured when thunderstorm winds caused a tree to fall on his vehicle on Rockwood Rd in Calloway County. Two more tornadoes formed in Trigg County, one an EF-1, and an EF-2 that produced its strongest damage south of Hopkinsville in Christian County. Another EF-1 tornado formed northwest of Bremen and tracked northeastward to Island in McLean County. Torrential rainfall rates led to a few reports of flash flooding in Western Kentucky during the morning to early afternoon hours. ||Heading into the evening, MLCAPE values at 1800CST were 2000-3500 J/kg in W Kentucky. For W Kentucky, effective bulk shear rose to 45-55 kts, with SRH around 200-400 m2/s2 and STP of 2-4. 0-1 km shear was 20-30 kts. Mid-level lapse rates were 7-7.5 C/km. DCAPE of 1100-1400 J/kg provided ample opportunity for downburst winds.||Late afternoon to early evening supercells produced hail reports and a long-track EF-3 tornado that began in Lyon County, then continued east-northeastward through Caldwell and Hopkins Counties. Severe structural damage occurred between Dawson Springs and Charleston and at least one family in Barnsley lost their home for the second time with the previous instance being the historic December 10, 2021 tornado. 21 people were injured (4 in Caldwell, 17 in Hopkins) while two people lost their lives, one in Hopkins County from the tornado (48f, direct), and one in Caldwell County due to a heart attack while cleaning up tornado debris (54m, indirect). The cold front swept through, producing more straight-line wind damage and flash flooding. As the line caught up to the supercell tornado, the storm became much more muddled, with an EF-1 tornado taking shape in Muhlenberg County tracking southeastward through Beech Creek and Beechmont, and continuing as a long track tornado reaching areas north of Bowling Green. Late night power outages were widespread along the tornado tracks in Lyon, Hopkins, Caldwell, and Muhlenberg Counties, which other counties with around half the county without power included Calloway, Trigg, Christian, and Todd Counties. Rainfall amounts of 2.5 to 4 inches were commonplace, with locally higher totals in southern Trigg and Christian Counties. Brief minor flooding occurred for the third time this month on part of the S. Fork Little River near Hopkinsville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail estimated to quarter size was observed north of Calvert City as a supercell affected far northern Marshall County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system passing south rapidly moved east during the evening of the 16th. Widespread moderate to heavy snow north of the system was found across the region, generally along and south of the I-70 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observers southeast of Oakbrook and east of Union both measured 2.5 inches of snow. The CVG ASOS augmentation measured 2.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A blast of winter weather cross through northeast Kentucky during the late evening hours of January 18th under the influence of a strong low pressure system. Even after its departure, northwesterly flow invited moisture and cold air from the Great Lakes region to progress downward and promoted continued snow showers through the day on the 19th and into the morning hours of January 20th. Generally 2 to 4 inches of snow fell across eastern Kentucky during this time.

An arctic airmass had previously invaded the Ohio Valley just a couple days prior to this heavy snowfall event. Bitterly cold air and breezy winds at times dropped wind chills below zero. From the 14th into the 15th of January, a couple weak upper level shortwave troughs supplied enhanced mid-level moisture to the region while increased meridional flow allowed more low-level moisture to advect northward from the Gulf of Mexico. Sufficient lift beneath the exit region of strong mid- and upper-level jet streaks allowed widespread snowfall to develop in two waves Sunday evening through Monday evening. The heavier snow bands set up across south-central Kentucky, with 3 to 6 inches of accumulation roughly south of a line from Bowling Green to Richmond. Localized 6+ inch totals were noted in the Lake Cumberland region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall accumulation of 3 to 5 inches occurred across Adair County ending the evening of January 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing temperatures were observed over most of western Kentucky during the early morning hours on the 6th. The lowest temperature recorded was 28 degrees in Henderson county. Temperatures were more variable near the freezing mark in the Kentucky Purchase region, but surface conditions were still favorable for frost formation. An area of high pressure allowed for mostly clear skies and calm winds. Any unprotected crops, vegetation, shrubs, and trees were late enough in the growing season to be impacted by the freezing temperatures and frost. Communities had also began turning on outdoor sprinkler irrigation systems for the warm season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in an unseasonably warm and humid airmass ahead of an approaching cold front.

A strong push of cold NW flow continued across the Commonwealth on the heels of the last big snowfall and frigid temperature event several days before. A large trough and shortwave were in place aloft across the region, and a cold front/low pressure system was approaching the state from the west on the 18th (Thursday). That cold front passed fairly quickly across the state Thursday night and into, having exited east by 12Z on Friday morning (19th), but left lingering NW upslope flow. Light snow and a wintry mix were expected across the region in response to this cold forcing. Initially winter weather advisories were put out for all of eastern Kentucky from 7pm Thursday the 18th until 7am Saturday the 20th. Soon after Harlan, Letcher, and Pike Counties were upgraded to Winter Storm Warnings for the same time frame. The higher elevations of these 3 counties were in line with the upslope flow and therefore were expecting more in the way of straight snow. The ironic part was that where highest totals were expected, actually underperformed. None of the counties in the warning met criteria. Meanwhile, despite only having winter weather advisories across much of JKL's area, there were a handful of counties that over-performed and reached warning criteria on January 19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The reports of snowfall started coming in for Rowan County on the morning of the 19th. At 7am, a cooperative observer located 2 miles SSE of Farmers reported 4 inches. By 7:30am, a member of the public in Morehead reported 4.5 inches on social media. Then by 10am, the total was up to 6 inches in Morehead. At 12:30pm, 5 inches was reported by the emergency manager 2 miles NNE of the Lakeview Heights area. Shortly after, the community of Hilda also reported 6.0 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 10am on the 19th, an NWS employee reported 3.5 inches of snow 1 mile S of Oggs Station, just shy of warning criteria. However as snow continued to fall, by noon the public made a report in a similar location (1 mile SW of Oggs Station) of 4.0 inches measured - officially reaching winter warning criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning on the 30th a frontal passage approached Northwest Kentucky from the northwest. Southerly winds around 5-10 mph ahead of the front tended to lift temperatures above freezing ahead of the fronts' approach in locations further west. Northwest Kentucky, however, had cooler early AM low temperatures of 28-30, which resulted in a couple hours of freezing drizzle near dawn. The light icing resulted in hazardous conditions and several slide-offs and collisions. One person died as a result of icy roads causing a head-on collision at the Livermore Bridge in McLean County. Temperatures warming well above freezing later in the morning brought an end to the hazardous conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to icy road conditions on the Livermore Bridge, a truck lost control and slid into on-coming traffic shortly after 5:30AM, hitting a car. The driver of the car (54f) was trapped and had to be extracted from the vehicle. First responders were able to get her out and attempt life-saving measures but, ultimately, she passed away (indirect) at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration cold weather event continued as broad high pressure remained over a large portion of the eastern half of the country. Wind chills early the 17th bottomed out around -10 with lows of 0-5 in Northwestern Kentucky. One person died as a result of cold weather exposure in Daviess County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Daviess County Coroner reported that a homeless woman (58f) living in her car passed away on the 17th due to exposure to cold weather. Temperatures were frigid on the 17th, with an early morning low of 1 in Owensboro with wind chills bottoming out at -11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic front moved through the Quad State region in the evening hours of the 18th through the early AM hours of the 19th. Ahead of the front, overrunning precipitation early morning on the 18th produced a wintry mix in the far southeastern Missouri counties and along the KY border with TN. Sleet and freezing drizzle reports were particularly numerous in Graves and Calloway counties during the morning on the 18th, with issues continuing into the evening as a car collided with a power pole which fell on it on KY-94 in Graves County. Evening instances of slide-offs were also reported from Christian and Muhlenberg counties. The overnight hours through the morning of the 19th saw a shift to northwesterly winds with additional light mixed precipitation, primarily affecting northwestern Kentucky and Southwest Indiana while frigid temperatures resulted in black ice issues.

An Arctic front moved through the Quad State region in the evening hours of the 18th through the early AM hours of the 19th. Ahead of the front, overrunning precipitation early morning on the 18th produced a wintry mix in the far southeastern Missouri counties and along the KY border with TN. Sleet and freezing drizzle reports were particularly numerous in Graves and Calloway counties during the morning on the 18th, with issues continuing into the evening as a car collided with a power pole which fell on it on KY-94 in Graves County. Evening instances of slide-offs were also reported from Christian and Muhlenberg counties. The overnight hours through the morning of the 19th saw a shift to northwesterly winds with additional light mixed precipitation, primarily affecting northwestern Kentucky and Southwest Indiana while frigid temperatures resulted in black ice issues.

Early morning on the 30th a frontal passage approached Northwest Kentucky from the northwest. Southerly winds around 5-10 mph ahead of the front tended to lift temperatures above freezing ahead of the fronts' approach in locations further west. Northwest Kentucky, however, had cooler early AM low temperatures of 28-30, which resulted in a couple hours of freezing drizzle near dawn. The light icing resulted in hazardous conditions and several slide-offs and collisions. One person died as a result of icy roads causing a head-on collision at the Livermore Bridge in McLean County. Temperatures warming well above freezing later in the morning brought an end to the hazardous conditions.

Freezing temperatures were observed over most of western Kentucky during the early morning hours on the 6th. The lowest temperature recorded was 28 degrees in Henderson county. Temperatures were more variable near the freezing mark in the Kentucky Purchase region, but surface conditions were still favorable for frost formation. An area of high pressure allowed for mostly clear skies and calm winds. Any unprotected crops, vegetation, shrubs, and trees were late enough in the growing season to be impacted by the freezing temperatures and frost. Communities had also began turning on outdoor sprinkler irrigation systems for the warm season.

While temperatures were highly variable with many locations remaining above freezing, surface conditions were still favorable for localized frost formation. High pressure had moved over the region that night and allowed for mostly clear skies and calm winds. Impacts were fairly isolated and minimal due to the short duration.

A very slow moving cold front was lined up west of the state during the day on Friday May 14th. A line of storms developed along this cold front and raced eastward ahead of the front, making it into eastern KY during the overnight hours. Just after 1am EDT on Friday March 15, the line of storms reached the far northern counties covered by NWS Jackson, producing a couple instances of wind damage in Montgomery and Bath Counties as they moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montgomery County Dispatch reported a tree blown down by thunderstorm winds. The tree was blocking Nest Egg Road near the intersection of Summer Ridge Road.

Just after 1am EDT on Friday March 15, the line of storms reached the far northern counties covered by NWS Jackson, producing a couple instances of wind damage in Montgomery and Bath Counties as they moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bath County dispatch reported a tree blown down onto telephone lines near the intersection of US-60 and Moores Ferry Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours on the 5th, a slow-moving frontal band of showers and thunderstorms oriented SW to NE was drifting southeastward across the Ohio River. A thunderstorm developed in SE portions of Henderson County, moving northwestward. As the storm collided with the line, locally heavy rainfall occurred over the city of Henderson, which led to flooding issues necessitating the closure of multiple roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water over the roadways in Henderson led to the closure of several roads and streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With strong low pressure tracking northeastward from Wisconsin towards Lake Superior, southwesterly wind gusts up to around 40 mph occurred in northwest Kentucky during the late morning to early afternoon hours on the 26th as the primary cold front was making its approach.

Strong surface low pressure was moving through Kansas towards Iowa with a cold front stretching southward. Strong wind gusts of 40-50 mph were seen through the day into the evening in Western Kentucky with the strongest measured gust 48 mph at the Henderson County KY Mesonet site. Wind speeds decreased but remained breezy in the evening as showers moved into W Kentucky, with a boundary moving through late evening into the overnight hours.

Freezing temperatures were observed in portions of western Kentucky during the early morning hours on the 22nd. The lowest temperature recorded was 30 degrees in Trigg and Todd counties. While temperatures were highly variable with many locations remaining above freezing, surface conditions were still favorable for localized frost formation. High pressure had moved over the region that night and allowed for mostly clear skies and calm winds. Any unprotected crops, vegetation, shrubs, and trees were late enough in the growing season to be impacted by the freezing temperatures and frost. Impacts were fairly isolated and minimal due to the short duration.

Temperatures were more variable near the freezing mark in the Kentucky Purchase region, but surface conditions were still favorable for frost formation. An area of high pressure allowed for mostly clear skies and calm winds. Any unprotected crops, vegetation, shrubs, and trees were late enough in the growing season to be impacted by the freezing temperatures and frost. Communities had also began turning on outdoor sprinkler irrigation systems for the warm season.

A cold front, which stretched from Oklahoma to Central Illinois over to Northern Ohio, progressed slowly through the Quad State region, with an MCS moving through during the evening hours. An early afternoon SGF sounding placed MLCAPE at around 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 50-55 kts with 0-1 km shear of 30 kts with 200 m2/s2 SRH. In Western Kentucky, storms moved through during the late evening, with an additional line of storms forming ahead of the main line. Several wind damage reports occurred between Bardwell and Paducah, continuing into Graves County. More isolated wind and sub-severe hail reports occurred elsewhere in Western Kentucky. The most concentrated area of wind damage northwest of Paducah with a narrow line of enhanced straight-line winds due to a downburst. A spotter measured a 75 mph wind gust in Christian County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in the Cunningham area, with power outages occurring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breezy to strong wind gusts became common across northeast Kentucky on the afternoon of January 12th in the midst of a passing low pressure system. Gusts on the order of 30 to 35 miles per hour were noted that afternoon and evening, which resulted in a few areas of tree damage and power outages. Winds began to ease off on the morning of January 13th once the low level jet associated with the system migrated to the northeast.

Arctic air and a winter storm kicked off the 3rd week of January 2024. Arctic air seeped into eastern Kentucky on Sunday, January 14th, following the passage of a shallow arctic cold front. That front eventually stalled along the spine of the Appalachians by late in the day. Temperatures fell into the 10s and 20s behind the front -- more than cold enough for snow. The first of multiple disturbances, passing through a deep upper level trough over the much of the Lower 48 east of the Rockies, induced moist and relatively warm air to overrun the cold arctic air mass by Sunday evening, causing snow to develop across eastern Kentucky during the evening of the 14th. Light to moderate snowfall continued into Monday morning with the heaviest amounts, exceeding 4 inches at some locations, falling between the Hal Roger Parkway/KY-80 corridor and the Mountain Parkway. Temperatures remained cold, only ranging in the teens to middle 20s. After periods of lighter snow for most of the day on Monday, the 15th, another disturbance caused steadier snow to develop from the southwest on Monday evening. The snow eventually tapered from the west on Tuesday morning, the 16th. The heavier snowfall with this second round was generally focused south of the Mountain Parkway with some locations again picking up in excess of 4 inches. Many locations saw snowfall tallies approaching a half foot or more. The highest snowfall report was 9.3 inches about 4 miles northeast of Booneville.||As the storm moved away on Tuesday, the arctic air remained entrenched over eastern Kentucky as strong high pressure built northeastward from the from the Tennessee Valley. Temperatures remained largely in the 10s to lower 20s through Tuesday afternoon once the snowfall ended. With minimal cloud cover and light wind, temperatures dropped quickly after sunset Tuesday evening, especially in valleys with deep snow cover. By early Wednesday morning, the 17th, many locations were below zero, including below -10��F in many of the sheltered valleys. The Monticello Airport took first place as the coldest location in eastern Kentucky, bottoming out at frigid -22��F. In fact, Monticello not only registered the coldest temperature for the Commonwealth, but also the entire Continental United States for that night.||Unfortunately a handful of deaths were reported across the state during this time, including one lady in Floyd County on January 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While no storm total snowfall reports were made on January 16, there were a couple reports from the day before that exceeded warning criteria. 4.0 inches of snow was reported by the pubic at 9:45am 1 mile NNE of Hollonville (Terrell Fork Road Community). Later that day, broadcast media reported 6.1 inches of snow at 8:45pm in the community of Bethany.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of cold NW flow continued across the Commonwealth on the heels of the last big snowfall and frigid temperature event several days before. A large trough and shortwave were in place aloft across the region, and a cold front/low pressure system was approaching the state from the west on the 18th (Thursday). That cold front passed fairly quickly across the state Thursday night and into, having exited east by 12Z on Friday morning (19th), but left lingering NW upslope flow. Light snow and a wintry mix were expected across the region in response to this cold forcing. Initially winter weather advisories were put out for all of eastern Kentucky from 7pm Thursday the 18th until 7am Saturday the 20th. Soon after Harlan, Letcher, and Pike Counties were upgraded to Winter Storm Warnings for the same time frame. The higher elevations of these 3 counties were in line with the upslope flow and therefore were expecting more in the way of straight snow. The ironic part was that where highest totals were expected, actually underperformed. None of the counties in the warning met criteria. Meanwhile, despite only having winter weather advisories across much of JKL's area, there were a handful of counties that over-performed and reached warning criteria on January 19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of between 2 and 3 inches of snow came in during the morning hours in Estill County. However, as the snow continued to fall in some locations, a member of the public located 1 mile NNW of Evelyn sent in a picture via social media of 6 inches haven fallen as of 12:55pm. Meanwhile, another location in the county (Weedon) only had 3.3 inches as of 9:45pm that evening - showing the variability across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of cold NW flow continued across the Commonwealth on the heels of the last big snowfall and frigid temperature event several days before. A large trough and shortwave were in place aloft across the region, and a cold front/low pressure system was approaching the state from the west on the 18th (Thursday). That cold front passed fairly quickly across the state Thursday night and into, having exited east by 12Z on Friday morning (19th), but left lingering NW upslope flow. Light snow and a wintry mix were expected across the region in response to this cold forcing. Initially winter weather advisories were put out for all of eastern Kentucky from 7pm Thursday the 18th until 7am Saturday the 20th. Soon after Harlan, Letcher, and Pike Counties were upgraded to Winter Storm Warnings for the same time frame. The higher elevations of these 3 counties were in line with the upslope flow and therefore were expecting more in the way of straight snow. The ironic part was that where highest totals were expected, actually underperformed. None of the counties in the warning met criteria. Meanwhile, despite only having winter weather advisories across much of JKL's area, there were a handful of counties that over-performed and reached warning criteria on January 19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer located near Olive Hill (3 miles ENE of Stark) measured 5 inches of snow as of 9am on January 19. This was the only report that was made for Elliott County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air and a winter storm kicked off the 3rd week of January 2024. Arctic air seeped into eastern Kentucky on Sunday, January 14th, following the passage of a shallow arctic cold front. That front eventually stalled along the spine of the Appalachians by late in the day. Temperatures fell into the 10s and 20s behind the front -- more than cold enough for snow. The first of multiple disturbances, passing through a deep upper level trough over the much of the Lower 48 east of the Rockies, induced moist and relatively warm air to overrun the cold arctic air mass by Sunday evening, causing snow to develop across eastern Kentucky during the evening of the 14th. Light to moderate snowfall continued into Monday morning with the heaviest amounts, exceeding 4 inches at some locations, falling between the Hal Roger Parkway/KY-80 corridor and the Mountain Parkway. Temperatures remained cold, only ranging in the teens to middle 20s. After periods of lighter snow for most of the day on Monday, the 15th, another disturbance caused steadier snow to develop from the southwest on Monday evening. The snow eventually tapered from the west on Tuesday morning, the 16th. The heavier snowfall with this second round was generally focused south of the Mountain Parkway with some locations again picking up in excess of 4 inches. Many locations saw snowfall tallies approaching a half foot or more. The highest snowfall report was 9.3 inches about 4 miles northeast of Booneville.||As the storm moved away on Tuesday, the arctic air remained entrenched over eastern Kentucky as strong high pressure built northeastward from the from the Tennessee Valley. Temperatures remained largely in the 10s to lower 20s through Tuesday afternoon once the snowfall ended. With minimal cloud cover and light wind, temperatures dropped quickly after sunset Tuesday evening, especially in valleys with deep snow cover. By early Wednesday morning, the 17th, many locations were below zero, including below -10��F in many of the sheltered valleys. The Monticello Airport took first place as the coldest location in eastern Kentucky, bottoming out at frigid -22��F. In fact, Monticello not only registered the coldest temperature for the Commonwealth, but also the entire Continental United States for that night.||Unfortunately a handful of deaths were reported across the state during this time, including one lady in Floyd County on January 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bell County had one storm total snowfall observation during the event - 2 miles SSW of Beverly first reported having met warning criteria with 4.0 inches of snow measured at 7pm on 15th. By the next morning, the storm total snowfall was 4.8 inches at the same location.

Temperatures remained cold, only ranging in the teens to middle 20s. After periods of lighter snow for most of the day on Monday, the 15th, another disturbance caused steadier snow to develop from the southwest on Monday evening. The snow eventually tapered from the west on Tuesday morning, the 16th. The heavier snowfall with this second round was generally focused south of the Mountain Parkway with some locations again picking up in excess of 4 inches. Many locations saw snowfall tallies approaching a half foot or more. The highest snowfall report was 9.3 inches about 4 miles northeast of Booneville.||As the storm moved away on Tuesday, the arctic air remained entrenched over eastern Kentucky as strong high pressure built northeastward from the from the Tennessee Valley. Temperatures remained largely in the 10s to lower 20s through Tuesday afternoon once the snowfall ended. With minimal cloud cover and light wind, temperatures dropped quickly after sunset Tuesday evening, especially in valleys with deep snow cover. By early Wednesday morning, the 17th, many locations were below zero, including below -10��F in many of the sheltered valleys. The Monticello Airport took first place as the coldest location in eastern Kentucky, bottoming out at frigid -22��F. In fact, Monticello not only registered the coldest temperature for the Commonwealth, but also the entire Continental United States for that night.||Unfortunately a handful of deaths were reported across the state during this time, including one lady in Floyd County on January 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer 4 miles N of Beattyville reported a total of 5.5 inches at 9:16am on January 16. This was an increase from 3.5 inches reported the morning before on the 15th around the same time. Meanwhile, there were also 2 additional reports given on January 15 with some pretty high amounts. At 12:45pm, public reported 6.0 inches of snow 1 mile ESE of Athol. Then at 7:35pm, there was a public report of 7.0 inches of snow measured in the community of Athol on Highland Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms moved across central and southern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours on May 3rd. One particular cell remained across portions of LaRue County for an extended period of time, resulting in heavy rainfall totals. This heavy rainfall resulted in minor flash flooding along U.S. Route 31E in northern LaRue County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused Knob Creek to overflow its banks near Abraham Lincoln's boyhood home in northern LaRue County. Debris from flood waters washed into Bardstown Road (U.S. Route 31E), blocking it for a short time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In northeast Kentucky, a cold frontal passage took place on the evening of January 9th, preceding a strong winter storm that took place east of the Ohio River. Strong winds attached to this storm contributed to widespread power outages due to downed trees and power lines. This was at the result of a decent low level jet accompanying the disturbance. Initial strong wind gust observations took place by the late afternoon hours on the 9th across northeast Kentucky as daytime heating mixed down the higher winds from aloft. ||The strongest measured wind gust took place at a mesonet station in Boyd County of 49 miles per hour. In Greenup County, multiple areas of downed trees were observed from the evening of the 9th to the morning of the 10th in the midst of the strong winds. In the wake of the cold front, winds began to diminish on the morning of January 10th. Power restoration efforts continued into the end of the day.

A rapidly deepening low pressure system, with one of the lowest pressure values to occur in January in this region, tracked from near St. Louis towards Chicago during the day on the 12th. Heavy rain and thunderstorms moved through Western Kentucky during the morning, with the stronger wind gusts following the heavy rain, primarily out of the west during the afternoon to evening hours. The strongest gust recorded in Western Kentucky was 51 mph at the Paducah ASOS. The two damage reports received were in far Western Kentucky, with a large tree at Noble Park in Paducah falling, and power outages near La Center in Ballard County.

A rapidly deepening low pressure system, with one of the lowest pressure values to occur in January in this region, tracked from near St. Louis towards Chicago during the day on the 12th. Precipitation streamed east-northeastward across the Tennessee River Valley on the 14th and 15th. The northern fringes of this system crossed the Quad State in two primary waves, one on the 14th during the afternoon into the early evening, and the second on the 15th during the morning. The first wave on the 14th resulted in 1-2'' of snowfall in Western Kentucky. The second wave on the 15th resulted in an additional 1-2'' of snowfall in Western Kentucky. The highest reported 2-day total was 4.0 inches 1 mile ENE of Hickman in Fulton County.

With surface low pressure well to the southeast and continued northwesterly flow of Arctic air, clear skies and fresh snow cover from the 1-4'' of snow that fell the 14th-15th in Western Kentucky allowed for frigid temperatures and wind chill values. Lows dropped to 4-9 degrees across Western Kentucky. Wind chills were -5 or lower, with Henderson the standout relative to advisory criteria.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over Cavitt Rd in Kirksey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of high pressure moved over the Quad State region and surrounding areas as a cold front pushed off the Gulf and Atlantic coasts. Snow cover was very limited after mixed precipitation on the 18th-19th but northwesterly winds of 5-10 mph remained in the early morning hours. Lows in Western Kentucky early on the 20th dropped to 3-7 degrees with wind chills generally -5 to -10. Wind chills below -10 occurred in Henderson/Daviess counties.

An Arctic front moved through the Quad State region in the evening hours of the 18th through the early AM hours of the 19th. Ahead of the front, overrunning precipitation early morning on the 18th produced a wintry mix in the far southeastern Missouri counties and along the KY border with TN. Sleet and freezing drizzle reports were particularly numerous in Graves and Calloway counties during the morning on the 18th, with issues continuing into the evening as a car collided with a power pole which fell on it on KY-94 in Graves County. Evening instances of slide-offs were also reported from Christian and Muhlenberg counties. The overnight hours through the morning of the 19th saw a shift to northwesterly winds with additional light mixed precipitation, primarily affecting northwestern Kentucky and Southwest Indiana while frigid temperatures resulted in black ice issues.

An Arctic front plunged across the Quad State region, bringing strong cold advection and breezy winds on the 14th, dropping wind chill values to around -10 to -15 across far northern portions of NW Kentucky, while the rest of Western Kentucky had wind chills bottom out around -5. These values were still quite low considering the lack of snow cover as only flurries occurred the evening prior.

The long duration fog event continued from the late afternoon of the 24th through the morning of the 25th. Dense fog formation of a quarter mile or less was most widespread in Western Kentucky during the late evening on the 24th through the morning on the 25th. Calm winds, light drizzle, and dew points in the lower 50s over a rapidly diminishing soil frost depth contributed to fog formation. This fog event had the largest coverage area in over a decade, with Dense Fog Advisories stretching from PA to ND to TX to portions of the FL Panhandle.

Well above normal rainfall along the Ohio River Valley in January led to rising rivers, with flood stage exceeded slightly across the river from Newburgh Lock and Dam, Mount Vernon, and Shawneetown by the end of the month, continuing into early February. Minor flooding resulted in very limited impacts in bottomland areas.

A very strong surface low, which set a January record for lowest pressure observed at Evansville, IN, advanced northeastward from St Louis towards Chicago during the day on the 9th. A cold front pushed through the Quad State with gusty winds ahead of the front from the south and behind the front from the west. Winds were gusty from the early afternoon through much of the overnight hours, with the strongest gusts around 35-45 mph generally occurring during the mid-afternoon hours. The strongest measured gust in Western Kentucky was 46 mph at the KY Mesonet stations 2 miles SE of Princeton and 6 miles N of Hopkinsville.

Despite rain moving through portions of Western Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours on the 23rd, dense fog of a quarter mile visibility redeveloped near the Ohio and Mississippi Rivers as a result of calm winds and dew points in the mid 40s to around 50, which drew in moisture from frozen near-surface soils. Locations further southeast maintained winds throughout, preventing dense fog formation. On a broader scale, a large-scale fog event was developing, stretching from the Upper Midwest to the Southern Plains.

On a broader scale, a large-scale fog event was developing, stretching from the Upper Midwest to the Southern Plains.

A rapidly deepening low pressure system, with one of the lowest pressure values to occur in January in this region, tracked from near St. Louis towards Chicago during the day on the 12th. Heavy rain and thunderstorms moved through Western Kentucky during the morning, with the stronger wind gusts following the heavy rain, primarily out of the west during the afternoon to evening hours. The strongest gust recorded in Western Kentucky was 51 mph at the Paducah ASOS. The two damage reports received were in far Western Kentucky, with a large tree at Noble Park in Paducah falling, and power outages near La Center in Ballard County.

Precipitation streamed east-northeastward across the Tennessee River Valley on the 14th and 15th. The northern fringes of this system crossed the Quad State in two primary waves, one on the 14th during the afternoon into the early evening, and the second on the 15th during the morning. The first wave on the 14th resulted in 1-2'' of snowfall in Western Kentucky. The second wave on the 15th resulted in an additional 1-2'' of snowfall in Western Kentucky. The highest reported 2-day total was 4.0 inches 1 mile ENE of Hickman in Fulton County.

In northeast Kentucky, a cold frontal passage took place on the evening of January 9th, preceding a strong winter storm that took place east of the Ohio River. Strong winds attached to this storm contributed to widespread power outages due to downed trees and power lines. This was at the result of a decent low level jet accompanying the disturbance. Initial strong wind gust observations took place by the late afternoon hours on the 9th across northeast Kentucky as daytime heating mixed down the higher winds from aloft. ||The strongest measured wind gust took place at a mesonet station in Boyd County of 49 miles per hour. In Greenup County, multiple areas of downed trees were observed from the evening of the 9th to the morning of the 10th in the midst of the strong winds. In the wake of the cold front, winds began to diminish on the morning of January 10th. Power restoration efforts continued into the end of the day.

On the afternoon of the 27th, a warm front had moved through and northeast of the forecast area, with a cold front situated to the northwest. Strong southerly winds advected plenty of moisture with dew points into the 60s at southern sites. Mid-level lapse rates were steep and 0-3 km helicity was several hundred m2/s2. However, a strong capping inversion suppressed storm development through the evening. The overnight passage of the cold front finally provided enough forcing for thunderstorm development. An additional line of storms ahead of the front formed in northwest Kentucky and tracked eastward from 0130 to 0330CST producing thunderstorm wind damage in Whitesville. The cold front storm line continued across the remainder of Western Kentucky through the remainder of the overnight hours with strong winds and sub-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple observers found hail estimated to be nickel-sized in Murray as a result of an early morning line of storms moving through.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retired NWS employee reported roof damage to a large metal storage shed and a covered back deck in Clear Springs which occurred as a result of the overnight line of storms that moved across Graves County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms developed in the early morning hours of February 10, 2024, ahead of a cold front in an environment that had very strong shear but little instability. The storms, mainly in southern Indiana and portions of north-central Kentucky, ended up morphing into a convective line that produced both wind damage and a couple of isolated tornadoes before weakening and pushing east of the region by sunrise. A total of two small, short-lived EF-1 tornadoes developed with the line, one in southern Indiana and one is central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Louisville conducted a storm damage survey near and in the|town of Port Royal and determined an EF-1 tornado with 90mph|winds impacted the community. The tornado first touched down east|of Gullion Branch Road and caused scattered tree damage. Damage in|these areas consisted of snapped tree trunks and broken limbs.|The tornado then moved into the town of Port Royal and did the most|significant damage near Port Royal Road. A pole barn was|completely destroyed, and its debris was carried more than a|quarter mile to the ENE. Several other structures had roof and|siding damage. Several trees were snapped or sheared apart, with|roof debris wrapping around some of the trees. Tree debris was in|a convergent pattern, with most of it pointing towards the SE to |NE. Nearby vehicles had mud splatter from the|tornado as it tore through that portion of the town. One vehicle|was picked up and moved several feet and turned 90 degrees. ||The tornado then pushed east of Port Royal Road and mainly |impacted trees. The observed damage was consistent with high end |EF0 to low end EF1 damage. The tornado then lifted in a field as |damage could no longer be observed by drone to the east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through eastern Kentucky during the morning hours of February 28. Strong winds aloft were able to mix down to the surface with the storms that formed along the boundary, leading to numerous trees down and high wind gusts across much of eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down along the 1000 block of Dry Creek Road near Clearfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain and a few storms traversed the area on the night of February 27th, then activity began to increase the morning of the 28th as a cold front slowly approached from the west. Strong to severe storms initially developed out ahead of a convective line during the early morning hours of the 28th. The front edge of the line pushed into Southeast Ohio and Northeastern Kentucky shortly before daybreak and then continued east across West Virginia into the early afternoon on the 28th.||In Kentucky, the strongest winds and most robust storm activity occurred along the leading edge of this line. One inch hail was reported in Carter County, and shortly after a wind gust of 61 miles per hour was measured in Boyd County. In addition to storms, one to one and a half inches of rain fell over soils that were already saturated from rain the previous day. Flash flooding occurred along some of the smaller creeks and streams during the morning, then Tygarts Creek exceeded its bankfull level and led to flooded roadways in the vicinity of the Olive Hill gauge. Rain exited to the east by the afternoon of the 28th and flooding came to an end that same evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry Fork exceeded its banks and caused flooding at the junction of Dry Fork Road and Aden Road.

Biggs Bridge Road and Bond Hollow Road both experienced flooding from Tygarts Creek.

A strong cold front moved through W Kentucky around 0230-0430CST on Feb 28th as strengthening low pressure moved towards the Great Lakes. Gusty gradient winds occurred ahead of the front during the late afternoon to evening hours and behind the front towards early morning. Thunderstorm activity in W Kentucky overnight was most organized with the frontal passage, though an additional band ahead of the front in far NW Kentucky produced strong to severe storms as well. The strongest measured gust in Kentucky was 43 mph at Madisonville Municipal Airport during the afternoon on the 27th.

On the afternoon of the 27th, a warm front had moved through and northeast of the forecast area, with a cold front situated to the northwest. Strong southerly winds advected plenty of moisture with dew points into the 60s at southern sites. Mid-level lapse rates were steep and 0-3 km helicity was several hundred m2/s2. However, a strong capping inversion suppressed storm development through the evening. The overnight passage of the cold front finally provided enough forcing for thunderstorm development. An additional line of storms ahead of the front formed in northwest Kentucky and tracked eastward from 0130 to 0330CST producing thunderstorm wind damage in Whitesville. The cold front storm line continued across the remainder of Western Kentucky through the remainder of the overnight hours with strong winds and sub-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EMA office in downtown Owensboro observed pea to dime size hail as the first of several late night storms tracked eastward across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of May 26, a stationary front had set up along the Ohio River just north of the state. During the course of the day, this front slowly lifted northward as a warm front, as a low pressure system began to develop and take shape just west of the state. This added forcing, combined with Kentucky's location in a warm/moist airmass, was enough to spark widespread showers and thunderstorms. WPC noted an outflow boundary on several surface analysis, the presence of which contributed to more shower and thunderstorm development. Most of the storms took place during the afternoon hours on the 26th, however, some storms and damage occurred as early as 3am EDT and as late as 2 and 3am EDT the following night. Amongst the many reports of thunderstorm wind damage, there was also a quick and small rotation that developed in the community of Corbin, which led to a EF-1 tornado, affecting a car dealership and several other businesses/homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down onto Mud Lick Road near the community of Beverly.

WPC noted an outflow boundary on several surface analysis, the presence of which contributed to more shower and thunderstorm development. Most of the storms took place during the afternoon hours on the 26th, however, some storms and damage occurred as early as 3am EDT and as late as 2 and 3am EDT the following night. Amongst the many reports of thunderstorm wind damage, there was also a quick and small rotation that developed in the community of Corbin, which led to a EF-1 tornado, affecting a car dealership and several other businesses/homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media report showed a large tree snapped at base on New Wilson Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in an unseasonably warm and humid airmass ahead of an approaching cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first developed in Jefferson County, Indiana, at 1258EST, on the north side of Hanover. The tornado moved east across the Ohio River into Trimble County, Kentucky, and then into Carroll County at 1308EST. ||There was extensive tree damage just inside of western Carroll County where winds were estimated to be 115 mph, EF-2 strength, before weakening across the rugged Fairview ridge. There was one large barn along Hampton Road that had extensive roof paneling damage and a door blown out. Wind speeds here were estimated to be 90 mph, EF-1. There was a lot of topped tree damage along Locust Creek and Kentucky State Route 36, where wind speeds were estimated to be 85 mph, EF-0.||At 1315EST, the tornado moved across the Ohio River again and back into Jefferson County, Indiana. The tornado continued east across southwest Switzerland County, Indiana, before crossing the Ohio River one more time and moving back into Carroll County, Kentucky, and ending at 1324EST, 2 miles east of Carrollton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and evening hours ahead of a low pressure system moving through the Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed north of the AA Highway, just west of Vanceburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second major severe weather outbreak for the month occurred on the 26th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a shortwave trough centered in the middle of the country with a 60 kt mid-level jet moved across northern Arkansas. A weak surface low was moving into SE Missouri during the morning with a secondary low located further northwest. A warm frontal boundary was draped across our area. A bowing line progressed east across SE Missouri into W Kentucky and far S Illinois between 0500-1000CST, followed by a mostly sub-severe second line that exited the region by 1300CST. The environment recovered producing fuel for more severe storms, starting with supercells 1800-2100CST with a line of storms moving east-southeastward across the entire Quad State after 1900CST, crossing through by midnight. Across the Quad State, this event produced the record for most tornado track miles in a single day in office history, along with the second most warnings issued in a day (just behind the May 8, 2024 event). Widespread straight-line wind and tornado damage resulted in over 100,000 customers without power across the Quad State.||For the morning, MLCAPE values at 0700CST were 1000-2000 J/kg in W Kentucky. Effective bulk shear was 30-40 kts with STP around 1, which increased in the following hours. Precipitable water was 1.5-1.8 inches, allowing for torrential rainfall rates.||The morning round of storms produced widespread destructive straight-line wind damage leading to numerous power outages across the southernmost portions of W Kentucky from the Jackson Purchase Area to the Southern Pennyrile. Two EF-1 tornadoes occurred in Calloway County, with one of them tracking 22 miles. One person was injured when thunderstorm winds caused a tree to fall on his vehicle on Rockwood Rd in Calloway County. Two more tornadoes formed in Trigg County, one an EF-1, and an EF-2 that produced its strongest damage south of Hopkinsville in Christian County. Another EF-1 tornado formed northwest of Bremen and tracked northeastward to Island in McLean County. Torrential rainfall rates led to a few reports of flash flooding in Western Kentucky during the morning to early afternoon hours. ||Heading into the evening, MLCAPE values at 1800CST were 2000-3500 J/kg in W Kentucky. For W Kentucky, effective bulk shear rose to 45-55 kts, with SRH around 200-400 m2/s2 and STP of 2-4. 0-1 km shear was 20-30 kts. Mid-level lapse rates were 7-7.5 C/km. DCAPE of 1100-1400 J/kg provided ample opportunity for downburst winds.||Late afternoon to early evening supercells produced hail reports and a long-track EF-3 tornado that began in Lyon County, then continued east-northeastward through Caldwell and Hopkins Counties. Severe structural damage occurred between Dawson Springs and Charleston and at least one family in Barnsley lost their home for the second time with the previous instance being the historic December 10, 2021 tornado. 21 people were injured (4 in Caldwell, 17 in Hopkins) while two people lost their lives, one in Hopkins County from the tornado (48f, direct), and one in Caldwell County due to a heart attack while cleaning up tornado debris (54m, indirect). The cold front swept through, producing more straight-line wind damage and flash flooding. As the line caught up to the supercell tornado, the storm became much more muddled, with an EF-1 tornado taking shape in Muhlenberg County tracking southeastward through Beech Creek and Beechmont, and continuing as a long track tornado reaching areas north of Bowling Green. Late night power outages were widespread along the tornado tracks in Lyon, Hopkins, Caldwell, and Muhlenberg Counties, which other counties with around half the county without power included Calloway, Trigg, Christian, and Todd Counties. Rainfall amounts of 2.5 to 4 inches were commonplace, with locally higher totals in southern Trigg and Christian Counties. Brief minor flooding occurred for the third time this month on part of the S. Fork Little River near Hopkinsville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts in La Center were estimated at 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A building roof was damaged and a few power poles were downed on State Route 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Patchy shallow dense fog formed over much of Western Kentucky near sunrise on the 18th. Surface winds were weak with high pressure moving in, while temperatures cooled to the crossover temperature. Surface moisture was plentiful with recent rainfall. Greater coverage/duration of fog occurred in southern portions of the Pennyrile. Cloud cover prevented fog formation in the southwestern half of the Jackson Purchase area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first developed in Jefferson County, Indiana, at 1258EST, on the north side of Hanover. The tornado moved east across the Ohio River into Trimble County, Kentucky, and then into Carroll County, Kentucky, at 1308EST.  The tornado moved across the Ohio River again and back into Jefferson County, Indiana, and then  Switzerland County, Indiana, before crossing the Ohio River one more time and moving back into Carroll County, Kentucky, at 1322EST, just east of Carrollton on US 42. The tornado was very narrow at this point and produced some sporadic tree damage before ending at 1324EST.  Wind speeds were estimated to be around 75 mph, EF-0. The Kentucky Mesonet site 2E of Carrollton measured 68 mph as the weakening tornado lifted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary moved through Western Kentucky and Tennessee the afternoon of the 22nd. The primary line of storms was in Tennessee while smaller scale SW to NE oriented heavy rain bands flared up three times in Western Kentucky. MLCAPE was 2000-3000 J/kg while DCAPE was 500-750 J/kg. Effective bulk shear was around 30-35 kts. Mid-level lapse rates were 6.5-7.5 C/km. Surface frontogenesis was strongest in Western Kentucky around 1400CST, after which heavy rain flared up. Severe weather was limited to wind damage in Marshall and Daviess Counties while training heavy rain in Webster County produced ponding on a road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains led to some standing water on a road outside Providence. 3 hour rainfall amounts in the area were estimated by radar to be 1.5-2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between a stalled frontal boundary and a disturbance crossing the region, several rounds of rain and storms impacted northeastern Kentucky between the 6th and 7th of May.  One and a half to two and a half inches of rain fell across portions of the area the evening and night of the 6th and led to flash flooding in the vicinity of Rush in Boyd County, in and around the city of Grayson in Carter County, between Argillite and Hunnewell in Greenup County, and along Nats Creek Road and Copper Road in Lawrence County. While flooding came to an end that night, waves of rain and storms continued on the 7th. One strong storm downed several trees outside the town of South Shore in Greenup County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South State Highway 1 was impassable due to high water at Damron Branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As of 0Z on May 8, a cold front was approaching Kentucky from the north and west. This front this proceeded to stall during the day and into the night. This placed all of Kentucky in a warm, moist, and unstable airmass, promoting showers and thunderstorms from morning through evening. The first round occurred during the morning, mainly affecting the southern portion of the state. As the day wore on, coverage increased across the entire state. Despite the numerous thunderstorms, damage later in the evening also was confined to the southwestern counties in the NWS Jackson, KY domain. Multiple trees and powerlines were felled across Wayne, Whitley, and Laurel counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was down on power lines along Hwy-167 near Patten Creek Road in the community of Mt. Pisgah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With yet another strong occluded low located across the north-central Conus, and a cold front stretching southward through the Mississippi Valley, Kentucky found itself in a warm and unstable airmass. Despite the overnight hours and cooling temps, storms persisted in parts of the Jackson, KY county warning area. One of these storms moved through Laurel County, resulting in a downed tree and isolated flash flooding after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car was reportedly swept away by flood waters at the 600 block of Rita Lane in Lily. The occupant had to be rescued from their vehicle. Time of event is based on time of report at 2:10 AM EDT.

Scattered afternoon convection became more numerous for the mid-afternoon hours in the northern Pennyrile. MLCAPE was around 1000 J/kg while shear was limited. Many of these showers had high rain rates due to PW of 1.5-1.75 inch, particularly along a west-east boundary that slowly drifted northward. Storm progression to the ENE brought extended periods of heavier rainfall from 1200-1600CST, with Hopkins and Daviess County experiencing some flash flooding as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads flooded in St Joseph and surrounding areas of Southwestern Daviess County including locations which have not flooded for a long time. Areas in this part of Daviess County received 1-2 inches of rain in 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in early May, culminating in the May 8th severe weather and flooding event, resulted in rising river levels on the Lower Ohio and Mississippi Rivers, with Olmsted, Cairo, and Hickman reaching minor flood stage in the following days. Minor flooding in Western Kentucky around these river sites lasted 5 to 8 days with impacts limited to low-lying areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred near Hickman mid-month in low-lying areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of May 26, a stationary front had set up along the Ohio River just north of the state. During the course of the day, this front slowly lifted northward as a warm front, as a low pressure system began to develop and take shape just west of the state. This added forcing, combined with Kentucky's location in a warm/moist airmass, was enough to spark widespread showers and thunderstorms. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving through the Ohio Valley produced widespread showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began along Brooksville-Powersville Road west of Hamilton Road. Tree damage was observed in the area along with some minor damage to a home east of Hamilton Road. Several barns were heavily damaged or destroyed in the area. Additional observed damage was located on Michaels Lane and McNamara Lane where several barns were damaged. The tornado continued eastward, crossing Brooksville-Germantown Road, causing damage to more barns, outbuildings, and trees. Based on radar data and observed damage provided by the public on social media, it is believe the tornado dissipated north of Brooksville-Germantown Road west of Locust Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving through the Ohio Valley produced widespread showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began within the town of Minerva, Kentucky causing tree and minor structural damage. Numerous trees were observed to be uprooted and snapped along Minerva Tuckahoe Road, consistent with EF1 intensity. Additionally, a  structure was damaged on T Wenz Road, which confirmed the estimated EF1 intensity, with considerable roof coverage loss and a partial collapse of the second story brick wall. Due to a lack of access along the rest of T Wenz Road and Lee Creek, it is unknown how many trees and builders were damaged. The last known sign of damage, a few trees uprooted, was observed along Tuckahoe Road. It is believed the tornado dissipated before the Ohio River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of May 26th, a bowing line of thunderstorms pressed into eastern Kentucky and caused widespread tree and power line damage. This line of convection had a history of producing strong wind gusts on the upwards of 60 miles per hour or greater as it crossed through the Tennessee Valley, and that remained the case during its journey through Greenup County. Strong downbursts with these storms set forth extensive tree damage throughout the forecast area. In Kentucky, the biggest impact of wind damage outside of downed trees and power lines occurred to a home in Raceland where a portion of the roof was blown off. Thunderstorms quickly raced east of the Ohio River that afternoon, becoming quiet once more across eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of a roof was blown off of a home in Raceland with trees blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and evening storms impacted eastern Kentucky on May 7, 2024. A couple of these storms resulted in severe thunderstorm warnings, though no reports of damage were found. However, one storm that moved over Natural Bridge State Park did put down some isolated high wind gusts, resulting in multiples trees being downed between the Campground and Hoedown Island. Thankfully no one was injured during this incident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed at Natural Bridge State Park near the Campground and Hoedown Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As of 0Z on May 8, a cold front was approaching Kentucky from the north and west. This front this proceeded to stall during the day and into the night. This placed all of Kentucky in a warm, moist, and unstable airmass, promoting showers and thunderstorms from morning through evening. The first round occurred during the morning, mainly affecting the southern portion of the state. As the day wore on, coverage increased across the entire state. Despite the numerous thunderstorms, damage later in the evening also was confined to the southwestern counties in the NWS Jackson, KY domain. Multiple trees and powerlines were felled across Wayne, Whitley, and Laurel counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person on social media reported a large tree down across KY-229 at the intersection with Robinson Creek Road. Electric wires were also down across the road as a result. Both lanes were blocked, with traffic using the Robinson Creek Church parking lot to detour around.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system was wrapping up and becoming occluded across the Upper Great Lakes and southern Ontario on the morning of May 22. As the system continued to strengthen and slowly move northeast, a cold front extending southward through much of the conus continued to track eastward towards the Commonwealth. The strong push of warm/unstable air into the region triggered showers and thunderstorms during the day, ahead of the approaching front. One of these storms that moved across Wayne County produced wind damage, severe hail, and an isolated instance of flash flooding. Meanwhile, in neighboring Pulaski County, public relayed a report to the media of 4 inches of rain falling within a 1 hour period as the storm passed through. Ironically, no additional reports of flooding were made here.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported downed onto a car in Monticello. Multiple other reports of trees downed across Monticello were also made on X.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With yet another strong occluded low located across the north-central Conus, and a cold front stretching southward through the Mississippi Valley, Kentucky found itself in a warm and unstable airmass. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service surveyed damage in the Siler |community, on the eastern edge of Corbin. They confirmed, with the|help of video, that a quick spin-up tornado occurred, starting in|the NW corner of the Legacy Chevrolet dealership's parking lot, |affecting a gas station and car wash, then moving ENE across |US-25E, into a manufactured shed retail lot. It then crossed into |a tree line and adjacent field, possibly lifting momentarily, |before resuming damage near Poplar Grove Road. It then traveled |between Lynn Camp High School and Lynn Camp Elementary School, |before dissipating. Notable damage included damage to to vehicles |in the Legacy Chevrolet parking lot. Multiple cars and trucks |were pushed forward or to the side. The line of trucks near the |front of the lot were pushed up over the curb and halfway into the |grass. Debris was located along the seal of the driver-side |window of multiple trucks, and quite a few vehicles lost their|back windshields either partially or completely. The gas cap |covers were pulled off of the trucks, with some found at the end of the|tornado path about 0.75 miles away. The neighboring car wash lost|the majority of its windows and incurred damage to all sides of |the building, causing it to be structurally unsound. Across the |street, sheds and barns were moved around and flipped, including a|metal carport being thrown into the tree line to the ENE. No damage|was noted along the neighboring field, however minor roof damage |and a fallen tree occurred just to the ENE, at which point the |path of damage picked back up with multiple trees down along |Poplar Grove Road. At the end of the tornado track, a south-facing|house addition had its roof and ceiling lifted off, landing in |it's entirety to the north near the Lynn Camp Elementary School, |while additional debris was thrown into the east facing lawn of |the school. ||The track of this storm started at Cumberland Falls State Park, |moving east-northeast, resulting in a path of straight-line wind |damage across northern Whitley County with numerous trees felled.|NWS Jackson, KY radar showed a tightening of circulation occurring|across the Corbin area, near where the tornado occurred. Further |wind damage was noted farther ENE of the tornado path in a wooded |area along Middlefork Richland Road and surrounding areas. These |were all indicative of straight-line winds as well.

WPC noted an outflow boundary on several surface analysis, the presence of which contributed to more shower and thunderstorm development. Most of the storms took place during the afternoon hours on the 26th, however, some storms and damage occurred as early as 3am EDT and as late as 2 and 3am EDT the following night. Amongst the many reports of thunderstorm wind damage, there was also a quick and small rotation that developed in the community of Corbin, which led to a EF-1 tornado, affecting a car dealership and several other businesses/homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported to be blown down across the community of Martin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several days of heavy rain from thunderstorms across the Ohio Valley and Mid-Atlantic region, water drained down the Ohio River. During the early morning hours of April 15th, the river gauge at Ashland denoted a rise above flood stage and remained the case until later that evening. This resulted in minor flooding in the Catlettsburg area during that time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ohio River at Ashland rose above flood stage shortly after midnight on the 15th of April. The river continued to rise to a crest of around 52 feet later that morning and then eased back below flood stage that evening. Minor flooding of roads in low lying areas near Catlettsburg occurred during the time the Ohio River was above its flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Calm conditions allowed for a surface inversion to develop during the pre-dawn hours on the 4th. Sufficient moisture resulted in the formation of dense fog in southern portions of Western Kentucky, though low clouds limited a more widespread fog coverage/duration.

Calm conditions allowed for a surface inversion to develop during the pre-dawn hours on the 4th. Sufficient moisture resulted in the formation of dense fog in southern portions of Western Kentucky, though low clouds limited a more widespread fog coverage/duration.
Thunderstorms developed in an unseasonably warm and humid airmass ahead of an approaching cold front.

On the afternoon of May 22nd, a cold front approached from the west and then stalled over Ohio during the day. Showers and thunderstorms spread into northeast Kentucky ahead of the front around midday and caused a path of tree damage through Greenup County. A storm survey conducted in this area found evidence of a microburst with winds up to 90 miles per hour within the Lloyd township, which uprooted and snapped healthy trees. Another piece of considerable damage occurred at the Raceland High School, where the outdoor scoreboard was torn apart due to the ravaging winds.||Heavy rain from this storm fell over a poor drainage area and caused the Russell Fire Department to flood. Flooding eased by the evening, though the continued presence of the front to the west allowed additional rounds of rain and storms to cross the area through late day hours and into the night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water reached a depth of 16 inches in the Russell Fire Department due to poor drainage issues.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees were blown down along State Route 7 in Maloneton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system and accompanying frontal boundary set forth multiple rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms from April 2nd to the 3rd. This first came in the form of an intense squall line on the morning of the 2nd, which raced through eastern Kentucky and produced straight-line wind damage and brief tornadoes. ||The strongest tornado surveyed in the aftermath of storms was found in Boyd County, with structure damage indicative of an EF2 tornado and maximum winds of up to 120 miles per hour. Another tornado was observed near the town of Westwood, where a single-wide trailer was flipped over and significant damage was noted of homes and structures. Numerous areas of tree and power line damage were also seen across northeast Kentucky due to damaging wind gusts associated with the squall line.||In addition to wind and tornado damage, heavy downpours associated with rounds of storms led to flooding across portions of northeast Kentucky on April 3rd. Several roads were closed during this time due to high water issues as adjacent creeks and streams rose out of their banks. In the span of these two days, between 3 to 5 inches of rain had fallen along the Ohio River basin from Pennsylvania, West Virginia, and Ohio, which eventually flowed down the river into Kentucky. The river gauge at Ashland eventually rose above its flood stage on April 6th and returned into its banks the next day as the water continued to flow downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down on Lowman Drive and Leighwood Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon convection became more numerous for the mid-afternoon hours in the northern Pennyrile. MLCAPE was around 1000 J/kg while shear was limited. Many of these showers had high rain rates due to PW of 1.5-1.75 inch, particularly along a west-east boundary that slowly drifted northward. Storm progression to the ENE brought extended periods of heavier rainfall from 1200-1600CST, with Hopkins and Daviess County experiencing some flash flooding as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding at a business with water almost up to the building. A radar estimated 1.5-3 inches of rainfall fell in Madisonville from 1200CST-1500CST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous areas of tree and power line damage was observed in the Raceland area. These included Armada Boulevard, Caroline Road, Rams Boulevard, Brown Street, Belmont Road, Meadow Avenue, and Pond Run Road. A few trees fell onto cars and roofs of homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of May 22nd, a cold front approached from the west and then stalled over Ohio during the day. Showers and thunderstorms spread into northeast Kentucky ahead of the front around midday and caused a path of tree damage through Greenup County. A storm survey conducted in this area found evidence of a microburst with winds up to 90 miles per hour within the Lloyd township, which uprooted and snapped healthy trees. Another piece of considerable damage occurred at the Raceland High School, where the outdoor scoreboard was torn apart due to the ravaging winds.||Heavy rain from this storm fell over a poor drainage area and caused the Russell Fire Department to flood. Flooding eased by the evening, though the continued presence of the front to the west allowed additional rounds of rain and storms to cross the area through late day hours and into the night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large thirty to forty foot maple tree was blown down on a residence near the town of Russell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain along the Green River basin from the May 23rd storms resulted in water levels rising at Paradise into minor flood on the 25th, while additional heavy rain from the May 26th storms caused a further rise to several feet above moderate flood stage at Paradise and a rise into minor flood at Calhoun. Flooding continued into the start of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of low-lying agricultural lands occurred. Flooding continued into the start of June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary moved through Western Kentucky and Tennessee the afternoon of the 22nd. The primary line of storms was in Tennessee while smaller scale SW to NE oriented heavy rain bands flared up three times in Western Kentucky. MLCAPE was 2000-3000 J/kg while DCAPE was 500-750 J/kg. Effective bulk shear was around 30-35 kts. Mid-level lapse rates were 6.5-7.5 C/km. Surface frontogenesis was strongest in Western Kentucky around 1400CST, after which heavy rain flared up. Severe weather was limited to wind damage in Marshall and Daviess Counties while training heavy rain in Webster County produced ponding on a road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm strengthened over Benton, eventually moving off to the east-northeast, producing wind damage in the city. Broadcast media relayed a report of a maple tree down southeast of Benton. A spotter measured a wind gust of 58 mph on the southwest side. Emergency management reported that a few large trees were down around Benton. Dime size hail also occurred with this storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One large oak tree and two smaller trees were downed by thunderstorm downdraft winds onto a house on Winkler Rd in Philpot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system and accompanying frontal boundary set forth multiple rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms from April 2nd to the 3rd. This first came in the form of an intense squall line on the morning of the 2nd, which raced through eastern Kentucky and produced straight-line wind damage and brief tornadoes. ||The strongest tornado surveyed in the aftermath of storms was found in Boyd County, with structure damage indicative of an EF2 tornado and maximum winds of up to 120 miles per hour. Another tornado was observed near the town of Westwood, where a single-wide trailer was flipped over and significant damage was noted of homes and structures. Numerous areas of tree and power line damage were also seen across northeast Kentucky due to damaging wind gusts associated with the squall line.||In addition to wind and tornado damage, heavy downpours associated with rounds of storms led to flooding across portions of northeast Kentucky on April 3rd. Several roads were closed during this time due to high water issues as adjacent creeks and streams rose out of their banks. In the span of these two days, between 3 to 5 inches of rain had fallen along the Ohio River basin from Pennsylvania, West Virginia, and Ohio, which eventually flowed down the river into Kentucky. The river gauge at Ashland eventually rose above its flood stage on April 6th and returned into its banks the next day as the water continued to flow downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A broken path of damage was noted on the Kentucky side of the tornado track, beginning at the Riverview Cemetery west of Greenup. Additional damage occurred on Seaton Avenue near South 5th Street and in the Greenup Fairgrounds, where a few buildings suffered roof damage and a large tree fell onto another building. The tornado then likely tracked along the Ohio River before intersecting land again on the Kentucky side and causing roof and tree damage to several homes along Calumet Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between a stalled frontal boundary and a disturbance crossing the region, several rounds of rain and storms impacted northeastern Kentucky between the 6th and 7th of May.  One and a half to two and a half inches of rain fell across portions of the area the evening and night of the 6th and led to flash flooding in the vicinity of Rush in Boyd County, in and around the city of Grayson in Carter County, between Argillite and Hunnewell in Greenup County, and along Nats Creek Road and Copper Road in Lawrence County. While flooding came to an end that night, waves of rain and storms continued on the 7th. One strong storm downed several trees outside the town of South Shore in Greenup County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple high water spots were observed within the city of Grayson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Patchy shallow dense fog formed over much of Western Kentucky near sunrise on the 18th. Surface winds were weak with high pressure moving in, while temperatures cooled to the crossover temperature. Surface moisture was plentiful with recent rainfall. Greater coverage/duration of fog occurred in southern portions of the Pennyrile. Cloud cover prevented fog formation in the southwestern half of the Jackson Purchase area.

A line of showers and storms developed ahead of an approaching cold front, moving across northeastern Kentucky in the afternoon. Additional storms formed ahead of the line, in far eastern Kentucky. Within the line of storms, one cell in particular became strong and resulted in severe thunderstorm warnings across multiple counties. This storm did result in straight line wind damage in portions of Rowan and Elliott Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rowan County dispatch reported a tree down on internet cables at the intersection of Knapp Avenue and Cecil Drive in Morehead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large scale ascent ahead of a shortwave moving in from Missouri was sufficient to generate a few early evening storms in the Evansville Tri-State area. MLCAPE was around 1500-2000 J/kg but effective bulk shear was very meager at around 20 kts. A few sub-severe hail reports came in with the largest being dime to nickel size hail in Henderson. Dime size hail also occurred in Stanley (Daviess County, KY) and just across the Ohio River in Yankeetown (Warrick County, IN).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report of hail estimated to be dime to nickel size in Henderson from a storm tracking to the ENE.
