EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major outbreak of severe weather occurred on the 8th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a longwave trough was centered across the Rockies with deep-layer southwesterly flow from the Southern Plains to the Ohio Valley. A 110 kt upper jet extended from northern KS to IA while a 60 kt mid-level jet was positioned across northern MZ into IA. Surface low pressure near the MZ/KS border lifted to St. Louis around 12am CST on the 9th. A warm frontal boundary stretched across the Quad State, with a cold front extending southward from the surface low. ||The environment became unstable by early afternoon, especially in far Western Kentucky, with MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg. Deep-layer shear was on the order of 55-65 kts, though low-level shear was 15 kts, increasing to 20 kts later in the day. SRH was around 100-2000 m2/s2 with higher values north towards Clarksville, TN. STP values were 2-3 due to deep-layer shear and instability. Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon in Western Kentucky with lapse rates of 6.5-7 C/km aiding with hail production.||For Southwest Kentucky, a sub-severe line of storms moved through with a cold front during the overnight hours from the 7th-8th. The front reversed and lifted northward during the morning, producing thunderstorm wind damage in the Purchase area, particularly in and around Calvert City. Supercell development occurred during the afternoon hours with widespread hail and thunderstorm wind damage. An EF-U tornado was identified in eastern McCracken County. Several counties were hit multiple times over the course of the afternoon into the evening as lines of storms entered Northwest Kentucky from Southern Illinois. Daviess County experienced widespread significant wind damage in and west of Owensboro between 2030-2100CST, including 3 tornadoes (2 EF-0 and 1 EF-1). A powerful outflow boundary from the Daviess County storms pushed through Livermore producing a mile wide swath of significant wind damage. A final line of thunderstorms pushed through Western Kentucky late evening past midnight, with limited severe weather. ||Torrential rainfall produced flooding impacts across Western Kentucky. A northern high rain total swath extended from Union County to southern Daviess/McLean Counties with 3-5 inches of rain. Being the location where the cold front stalled out and reversed, early morning rainfall totals were already elevated from Marshall/Calloway County to Todd County and further south in Tennessee. Heavy rains from afternoon supercells increased flooding issues, including washing out part of a road in Trigg County, while continued heavy rainfall late evening into the overnight hours left numerous roads flooded in Trigg County and in Hopkinsville. Rainfall totals were 5-8 inches in portions of Marshall and Trigg Counties and southernmost portions of Christian and Todd Counties. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of Thursday August the 1st, a warm front was draped across portions of the state. The airmass was warm across the state, and storms were already firing off across portions of the state, including eastern Kentucky by 7am. However, the real action would come later in the day. A cold front located across Illinois, still well away from Kentucky, produced a line of storms along it during the morning hours. This line of storms gained strength, becoming a squall line and quickly dropping southeast. It reached central Kentucky by the early evening hours, then eastern Kentucky a couple hours later. By the time it got to eastern Kentucky, the line began to lose it's form, but embedded cells still packed a punch, resulting in numerous warnings across the CWA and just as many reports of wind damage. It began to regain it's linear shape by the time it reached the far eastern portion of the state, finally exiting just between 10 and 11pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Employee reported that a tree had blown down across CR-3356 (Stamper Branch Road) near mile marker 1, just north of the community of Trent and west of Hazel Green.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another cold front impacted eastern Kentucky during the day on August 31st, rounding out the month. This time the cold front approached the state, then stalled out. Meanwhile, WPC still analyzed a surface boundary that continued eastward and through the state during the first half of the day. Then by the late afternoon hours, the actual front began to progress eastward again. This ultimately led to multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms across the region, starting with the initial boundary mid-day, and then ramping up in coverage by the afternoon hours ahead of the actual cold front. Multiple reports of severe winds and wind damage were reported across JKL's area, though Fleming, Morgan, and Johnson counties received the majority of the damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed on Hwy-7 and Hwy-460 in West Liberty. Exact locations along these roads were not given.

Several counties were hit multiple times over the course of the afternoon into the evening as lines of storms entered Northwest Kentucky from Southern Illinois. Daviess County experienced widespread significant wind damage in and west of Owensboro between 2030-2100CST, including 3 tornadoes (2 EF-0 and 1 EF-1). A powerful outflow boundary from the Daviess County storms pushed through Livermore producing a mile wide swath of significant wind damage. A final line of thunderstorms pushed through Western Kentucky late evening past midnight, with limited severe weather. ||Torrential rainfall produced flooding impacts across Western Kentucky. A northern high rain total swath extended from Union County to southern Daviess/McLean Counties with 3-5 inches of rain. Being the location where the cold front stalled out and reversed, early morning rainfall totals were already elevated from Marshall/Calloway County to Todd County and further south in Tennessee. Heavy rains from afternoon supercells increased flooding issues, including washing out part of a road in Trigg County, while continued heavy rainfall late evening into the overnight hours left numerous roads flooded in Trigg County and in Hopkinsville. Rainfall totals were 5-8 inches in portions of Marshall and Trigg Counties and southernmost portions of Christian and Todd Counties. Both the Bypass and 68/80 gages on the South Fork of the Little River in Hopkinsville reached minor flood, and significant rainfall totals on the Green River resulted in Paradise reaching moderate flood stage a couple days later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a result of thunderstorm downdraft winds, trees were downed near Cunningham with power outages reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system was in place across the Great Lakes region heading into the morning hours of Saturday, August 17. From this a cold front draped southwestward, moving towards the state throughout the day. By the time it reached western Kentucky during the afternoon hours. the warm/unstable airmass ahead of the boundary had already spawned showers and thunderstorms. A line of storms formed ahead of the front and continued to outpace the frontal passage eastward. By the time it reached eastern Kentucky, multiple severe thunderstorm warnings were issued for these strong storms. Throughout the afternoon and evening hours, widespread wind damage occurred in numerous counties. Multiple locations also saw damaging hail.  While the main line of storms fizzled out during the evening hours in the far east, additional discrete thunderstorms continued ahead and all the frontal boundary into the overnight for central and eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on KY-30 East, about 1 mile east of Airport Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure was drifting across the Quad State on the 14th. Showers and thunderstorms developed in the afternoon to evening with Western Kentucky seeing storms drift slowly, pivoting eastward to northeastward. CAPE values around 1000 J/kg dropped off after sunset. Lapse rates were around 6.5 C/km, and shear was limited to around 25 kts. A few of the storms became strong to severe with a tree falling onto a home near Eddyville and quarter size hail occurring on the south side of Princeton. Torrential rainfall of 3-5.5 inches fell in Henderson County, though flooding reports were confined to the western portions of the county. Flooding also occurred in Calloway County, with brief minor flooding in Christian County at the 68/80 Bypass site for the South Fork of the Little River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported near Princeton as a thunderstorm progressed eastward, with penny size hail near the Princeton Golf Club and quarter size hail on the south side of Princeton. A 56 mph wind gust was observed at the Mesonet station PRNC Princeton KY 2 SE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds during the day bringing in moist air lifting dew points to the mid to upper 70s resulted in heat index values reaching 105-109F for far Southwest Kentucky on the 15th and 16th. The highest heat index was 111F at the Murray-Calloway County Regional Airport AWOS on the 16th.

On the morning of Thursday August the 1st, a warm front was draped across portions of the state. The airmass was warm across the state, and storms were already firing off across portions of the state, including eastern Kentucky by 7am. However, the real action would come later in the day. A cold front located across Illinois, still well away from Kentucky, produced a line of storms along it during the morning hours. This line of storms gained strength, becoming a squall line and quickly dropping southeast. It reached central Kentucky by the early evening hours, then eastern Kentucky a couple hours later. By the time it got to eastern Kentucky, the line began to lose it's form, but embedded cells still packed a punch, resulting in numerous warnings across the CWA and just as many reports of wind damage. It began to regain it's linear shape by the time it reached the far eastern portion of the state, finally exiting just between 10 and 11pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Police reported several trees blown down onto Collins Highway near the intersection with Buckfield Road just north of the Yeager community.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moving northeastward into Christian County produced hail estimated to quarter size northwest of Fort Campbell.

On the morning of Thursday August the 1st, a warm front was draped across portions of the state. The airmass was warm across the state, and storms were already firing off across portions of the state, including eastern Kentucky by 7am. However, the real action would come later in the day. A cold front located across Illinois, still well away from Kentucky, produced a line of storms along it during the morning hours. This line of storms gained strength, becoming a squall line and quickly dropping southeast. It reached central Kentucky by the early evening hours, then eastern Kentucky a couple hours later. By the time it got to eastern Kentucky, the line began to lose it's form, but embedded cells still packed a punch, resulting in numerous warnings across the CWA and just as many reports of wind damage. It began to regain it's linear shape by the time it reached the far eastern portion of the state, finally exiting just between 10 and 11pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Perry county dispatch reported a tree down on Walker Branch road, blocking the highway and trapping power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system was in place across the Great Lakes region heading into the morning hours of Saturday, August 17. From this a cold front draped southwestward, moving towards the state throughout the day. By the time it reached western Kentucky during the afternoon hours. the warm/unstable airmass ahead of the boundary had already spawned showers and thunderstorms. A line of storms formed ahead of the front and continued to outpace the frontal passage eastward. By the time it reached eastern Kentucky, multiple severe thunderstorm warnings were issued for these strong storms. Throughout the afternoon and evening hours, widespread wind damage occurred in numerous counties. Multiple locations also saw damaging hail.  While the main line of storms fizzled out during the evening hours in the far east, additional discrete thunderstorms continued ahead and all the frontal boundary into the overnight for central and eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down and onto a powerline on Camp Judy Lane, north of Wellington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another cold front impacted eastern Kentucky during the day on August 31st, rounding out the month. This time the cold front approached the state, then stalled out. Meanwhile, WPC still analyzed a surface boundary that continued eastward and through the state during the first half of the day. Then by the late afternoon hours, the actual front began to progress eastward again. This ultimately led to multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms across the region, starting with the initial boundary mid-day, and then ramping up in coverage by the afternoon hours ahead of the actual cold front. Multiple reports of severe winds and wind damage were reported across JKL's area, though Fleming, Morgan, and Johnson counties received the majority of the damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station located 4 miles NW of Monticello measured a 61 mph wind gust at 6:45 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second major severe weather outbreak for the month occurred on the 26th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a shortwave trough centered in the middle of the country with a 60 kt mid-level jet moved across northern Arkansas. A weak surface low was moving into SE Missouri during the morning with a secondary low located further northwest. A warm frontal boundary was draped across our area. A bowing line progressed east across SE Missouri into W Kentucky and far S Illinois between 0500-1000CST, followed by a mostly sub-severe second line that exited the region by 1300CST. The environment recovered producing fuel for more severe storms, starting with supercells 1800-2100CST with a line of storms moving east-southeastward across the entire Quad State after 1900CST, crossing through by midnight. Across the Quad State, this event produced the record for most tornado track miles in a single day in office history, along with the second most warnings issued in a day (just behind the May 8, 2024 event). Widespread straight-line wind and tornado damage resulted in over 100,000 customers without power across the Quad State.||For the morning, MLCAPE values at 0700CST were 1000-2000 J/kg in W Kentucky. Effective bulk shear was 30-40 kts with STP around 1, which increased in the following hours. Precipitable water was 1.5-1.8 inches, allowing for torrential rainfall rates.||The morning round of storms produced widespread destructive straight-line wind damage leading to numerous power outages across the southernmost portions of W Kentucky from the Jackson Purchase Area to the Southern Pennyrile. Two EF-1 tornadoes occurred in Calloway County, with one of them tracking 22 miles. One person was injured when thunderstorm winds caused a tree to fall on his vehicle on Rockwood Rd in Calloway County. Two more tornadoes formed in Trigg County, one an EF-1, and an EF-2 that produced its strongest damage south of Hopkinsville in Christian County. Another EF-1 tornado formed northwest of Bremen and tracked northeastward to Island in McLean County. Torrential rainfall rates led to a few reports of flash flooding in Western Kentucky during the morning to early afternoon hours. ||Heading into the evening, MLCAPE values at 1800CST were 2000-3500 J/kg in W Kentucky. For W Kentucky, effective bulk shear rose to 45-55 kts, with SRH around 200-400 m2/s2 and STP of 2-4. 0-1 km shear was 20-30 kts. Mid-level lapse rates were 7-7.5 C/km. DCAPE of 1100-1400 J/kg provided ample opportunity for downburst winds.||Late afternoon to early evening supercells produced hail reports and a long-track EF-3 tornado that began in Lyon County, then continued east-northeastward through Caldwell and Hopkins Counties. Severe structural damage occurred between Dawson Springs and Charleston and at least one family in Barnsley lost their home for the second time with the previous instance being the historic December 10, 2021 tornado. 21 people were injured (4 in Caldwell, 17 in Hopkins) while two people lost their lives, one in Hopkins County from the tornado (48f, direct), and one in Caldwell County due to a heart attack while cleaning up tornado debris (54m, indirect). The cold front swept through, producing more straight-line wind damage and flash flooding. As the line caught up to the supercell tornado, the storm became much more muddled, with an EF-1 tornado taking shape in Muhlenberg County tracking southeastward through Beech Creek and Beechmont, and continuing as a long track tornado reaching areas north of Bowling Green. Late night power outages were widespread along the tornado tracks in Lyon, Hopkins, Caldwell, and Muhlenberg Counties, which other counties with around half the county without power included Calloway, Trigg, Christian, and Todd Counties. Rainfall amounts of 2.5 to 4 inches were commonplace, with locally higher totals in southern Trigg and Christian Counties. Brief minor flooding occurred for the third time this month on part of the S. Fork Little River near Hopkinsville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gust from the KCEY AWOS. Time estimated from radar; the observation was reported at 0735CST with 20 minute data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major outbreak of severe weather occurred on the 8th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a longwave trough was centered across the Rockies with deep-layer southwesterly flow from the Southern Plains to the Ohio Valley. A 110 kt upper jet extended from northern KS to IA while a 60 kt mid-level jet was positioned across northern MZ into IA. Surface low pressure near the MZ/KS border lifted to St. Louis around 12am CST on the 9th. A warm frontal boundary stretched across the Quad State, with a cold front extending southward from the surface low. ||The environment became unstable by early afternoon, especially in far Western Kentucky, with MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg. Deep-layer shear was on the order of 55-65 kts, though low-level shear was 15 kts, increasing to 20 kts later in the day. SRH was around 100-2000 m2/s2 with higher values north towards Clarksville, TN. STP values were 2-3 due to deep-layer shear and instability. Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon in Western Kentucky with lapse rates of 6.5-7 C/km aiding with hail production.||For Southwest Kentucky, a sub-severe line of storms moved through with a cold front during the overnight hours from the 7th-8th. The front reversed and lifted northward during the morning, producing thunderstorm wind damage in the Purchase area, particularly in and around Calvert City. Supercell development occurred during the afternoon hours with widespread hail and thunderstorm wind damage. An EF-U tornado was identified in eastern McCracken County. Several counties were hit multiple times over the course of the afternoon into the evening as lines of storms entered Northwest Kentucky from Southern Illinois. Daviess County experienced widespread significant wind damage in and west of Owensboro between 2030-2100CST, including 3 tornadoes (2 EF-0 and 1 EF-1). A powerful outflow boundary from the Daviess County storms pushed through Livermore producing a mile wide swath of significant wind damage. A final line of thunderstorms pushed through Western Kentucky late evening past midnight, with limited severe weather. ||Torrential rainfall produced flooding impacts across Western Kentucky. A northern high rain total swath extended from Union County to southern Daviess/McLean Counties with 3-5 inches of rain. Being the location where the cold front stalled out and reversed, early morning rainfall totals were already elevated from Marshall/Calloway County to Todd County and further south in Tennessee. Heavy rains from afternoon supercells increased flooding issues, including washing out part of a road in Trigg County, while continued heavy rainfall late evening into the overnight hours left numerous roads flooded in Trigg County and in Hopkinsville. Rainfall totals were 5-8 inches in portions of Marshall and Trigg Counties and southernmost portions of Christian and Todd Counties. Both the Bypass and 68/80 gages on the South Fork of the Little River in Hopkinsville reached minor flood, and significant rainfall totals on the Green River resulted in Paradise reaching moderate flood stage a couple days later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moving northeastward through Livermore caused large trees to be uprooted with a tree falling onto a house.

Several counties were hit multiple times over the course of the afternoon into the evening as lines of storms entered Northwest Kentucky from Southern Illinois. Daviess County experienced widespread significant wind damage in and west of Owensboro between 2030-2100CST, including 3 tornadoes (2 EF-0 and 1 EF-1). A powerful outflow boundary from the Daviess County storms pushed through Livermore producing a mile wide swath of significant wind damage. A final line of thunderstorms pushed through Western Kentucky late evening past midnight, with limited severe weather. ||Torrential rainfall produced flooding impacts across Western Kentucky. A northern high rain total swath extended from Union County to southern Daviess/McLean Counties with 3-5 inches of rain. Being the location where the cold front stalled out and reversed, early morning rainfall totals were already elevated from Marshall/Calloway County to Todd County and further south in Tennessee. Heavy rains from afternoon supercells increased flooding issues, including washing out part of a road in Trigg County, while continued heavy rainfall late evening into the overnight hours left numerous roads flooded in Trigg County and in Hopkinsville. Rainfall totals were 5-8 inches in portions of Marshall and Trigg Counties and southernmost portions of Christian and Todd Counties. Both the Bypass and 68/80 gages on the South Fork of the Little River in Hopkinsville reached minor flood, and significant rainfall totals on the Green River resulted in Paradise reaching moderate flood stage a couple days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another cold front impacted eastern Kentucky during the day on August 31st, rounding out the month. This time the cold front approached the state, then stalled out. Meanwhile, WPC still analyzed a surface boundary that continued eastward and through the state during the first half of the day. Then by the late afternoon hours, the actual front began to progress eastward again. This ultimately led to multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms across the region, starting with the initial boundary mid-day, and then ramping up in coverage by the afternoon hours ahead of the actual cold front. Multiple reports of severe winds and wind damage were reported across JKL's area, though Fleming, Morgan, and Johnson counties received the majority of the damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto State Hwy 581 (Tutor Key Road), near the S curve - or about a tenth of a mile north of KY-40 (Euclid Avenue) int eh community of Thealka, near Paintsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and storms formed in portions of Western Kentucky during the late afternoon and evening hours with storms remaining sub-severe. Several wind gusts of 40-45 mph were observed at KY Mesonet sites in Muhlenberg, Hopkins, and Todd Counties. In addition to numerous pieces of penny size hail north of Oak Grove, half inch hail in Powderly and dime size hail 6 miles SSE of Cypress in Hickman County were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous hailstones estimated at penny size were falling on KY-115 north of Oak Grove towards Pembroke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of Thursday August the 1st, a warm front was draped across portions of the state. The airmass was warm across the state, and storms were already firing off across portions of the state, including eastern Kentucky by 7am. However, the real action would come later in the day. A cold front located across Illinois, still well away from Kentucky, produced a line of storms along it during the morning hours. This line of storms gained strength, becoming a squall line and quickly dropping southeast. It reached central Kentucky by the early evening hours, then eastern Kentucky a couple hours later. By the time it got to eastern Kentucky, the line began to lose it's form, but embedded cells still packed a punch, resulting in numerous warnings across the CWA and just as many reports of wind damage. It began to regain it's linear shape by the time it reached the far eastern portion of the state, finally exiting just between 10 and 11pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Laurel county dispatch reported multiple trees blown down along KY-229 at different points between London and the Laurel/Knox county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system was in place across the Great Lakes region heading into the morning hours of Saturday, August 17. From this a cold front draped southwestward, moving towards the state throughout the day. By the time it reached western Kentucky during the afternoon hours. the warm/unstable airmass ahead of the boundary had already spawned showers and thunderstorms. A line of storms formed ahead of the front and continued to outpace the frontal passage eastward. By the time it reached eastern Kentucky, multiple severe thunderstorm warnings were issued for these strong storms. Throughout the afternoon and evening hours, widespread wind damage occurred in numerous counties. Multiple locations also saw damaging hail.  While the main line of storms fizzled out during the evening hours in the far east, additional discrete thunderstorms continued ahead and all the frontal boundary into the overnight for central and eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employee observed 1 inch hail at their house, just north of Jeffersonville and west of the community of Hope.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a quiet end to the month of July, exceptionally dry weather prevailed through the majority of August and attributed to a historical drought across the Central Appalachians. For eastern Kentucky, the month began with abnormally/moderate drought (D0/D1) conditions in Boyd and Lawrence Counties. Under strong high pressure and daily afternoon temperatures stretching into the triple digit marks, soil conditions continued to dry out. By the middle of August, these two counties were expanded to a severe drought (D2) state.||Declining levels in irritation wells/ponds in August led to hauling of water for livestock across the area. Other impacts from this drought also included a crop yield loss reported across much of the Ohio River Valley as well as nearby hay shortages. Given the drying vegetation and above normal temperatures, brush fire activity also increased by the end of the second half of the month. ||Exceptionally dry conditions stretched into September.

On the morning of Thursday August the 1st, a warm front was draped across portions of the state. The airmass was warm across the state, and storms were already firing off across portions of the state, including eastern Kentucky by 7am. However, the real action would come later in the day. A cold front located across Illinois, still well away from Kentucky, produced a line of storms along it during the morning hours. This line of storms gained strength, becoming a squall line and quickly dropping southeast. It reached central Kentucky by the early evening hours, then eastern Kentucky a couple hours later. By the time it got to eastern Kentucky, the line began to lose it's form, but embedded cells still packed a punch, resulting in numerous warnings across the CWA and just as many reports of wind damage. It began to regain it's linear shape by the time it reached the far eastern portion of the state, finally exiting just between 10 and 11pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Laurel county dispatch reported a tree down and electrical wires, leading to a fire on Skyline View, located on the southeast side of London.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system was in place across the Great Lakes region heading into the morning hours of Saturday, August 17. From this a cold front draped southwestward, moving towards the state throughout the day. By the time it reached western Kentucky during the afternoon hours. the warm/unstable airmass ahead of the boundary had already spawned showers and thunderstorms. A line of storms formed ahead of the front and continued to outpace the frontal passage eastward. By the time it reached eastern Kentucky, multiple severe thunderstorm warnings were issued for these strong storms. Throughout the afternoon and evening hours, widespread wind damage occurred in numerous counties. Multiple locations also saw damaging hail.  While the main line of storms fizzled out during the evening hours in the far east, additional discrete thunderstorms continued ahead and all the frontal boundary into the overnight for central and eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on KY-557 near Sandy Hook. The exact location on this road was not given.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a quiet end to the month of July, exceptionally dry weather prevailed through the majority of August and attributed to a historical drought across the Central Appalachians. For eastern Kentucky, the month began with abnormally/moderate drought (D0/D1) conditions in Boyd and Lawrence Counties. Under strong high pressure and daily afternoon temperatures stretching into the triple digit marks, soil conditions continued to dry out. By the middle of August, these two counties were expanded to a severe drought (D2) state.||Declining levels in irritation wells/ponds in August led to hauling of water for livestock across the area. Other impacts from this drought also included a crop yield loss reported across much of the Ohio River Valley as well as nearby hay shortages. Given the drying vegetation and above normal temperatures, brush fire activity also increased by the end of the second half of the month. ||Exceptionally dry conditions stretched into September.

A fairly small and weakening mesoscale convective system surged southeastward into far Western Kentucky during the evening hours of the 15th. MLCAPE was 2000 J/kg for the evening while effective bulk shear was 30 kts. Wind damage was observed in far northeastern Ballard County and western McCracken County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed in far NE Ballard County as a thunderstorm moved southeastward.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of Thursday August the 1st, a warm front was draped across portions of the state. The airmass was warm across the state, and storms were already firing off across portions of the state, including eastern Kentucky by 7am. However, the real action would come later in the day. A cold front located across Illinois, still well away from Kentucky, produced a line of storms along it during the morning hours. This line of storms gained strength, becoming a squall line and quickly dropping southeast. It reached central Kentucky by the early evening hours, then eastern Kentucky a couple hours later. By the time it got to eastern Kentucky, the line began to lose it's form, but embedded cells still packed a punch, resulting in numerous warnings across the CWA and just as many reports of wind damage. It began to regain it's linear shape by the time it reached the far eastern portion of the state, finally exiting just between 10 and 11pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Leslie county dispatch reported two trees blown down around mile marker 3 along US-421, in the community of Helton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major outbreak of severe weather occurred on the 8th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a longwave trough was centered across the Rockies with deep-layer southwesterly flow from the Southern Plains to the Ohio Valley. A 110 kt upper jet extended from northern KS to IA while a 60 kt mid-level jet was positioned across northern MZ into IA. Surface low pressure near the MZ/KS border lifted to St. Louis around 12am CST on the 9th. A warm frontal boundary stretched across the Quad State, with a cold front extending southward from the surface low. ||The environment became unstable by early afternoon, especially in far Western Kentucky, with MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg. Deep-layer shear was on the order of 55-65 kts, though low-level shear was 15 kts, increasing to 20 kts later in the day. SRH was around 100-2000 m2/s2 with higher values north towards Clarksville, TN. STP values were 2-3 due to deep-layer shear and instability. Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon in Western Kentucky with lapse rates of 6.5-7 C/km aiding with hail production.||For Southwest Kentucky, a sub-severe line of storms moved through with a cold front during the overnight hours from the 7th-8th. The front reversed and lifted northward during the morning, producing thunderstorm wind damage in the Purchase area, particularly in and around Calvert City. Supercell development occurred during the afternoon hours with widespread hail and thunderstorm wind damage. An EF-U tornado was identified in eastern McCracken County. Several counties were hit multiple times over the course of the afternoon into the evening as lines of storms entered Northwest Kentucky from Southern Illinois. Daviess County experienced widespread significant wind damage in and west of Owensboro between 2030-2100CST, including 3 tornadoes (2 EF-0 and 1 EF-1). A powerful outflow boundary from the Daviess County storms pushed through Livermore producing a mile wide swath of significant wind damage. A final line of thunderstorms pushed through Western Kentucky late evening past midnight, with limited severe weather. ||Torrential rainfall produced flooding impacts across Western Kentucky. A northern high rain total swath extended from Union County to southern Daviess/McLean Counties with 3-5 inches of rain. Being the location where the cold front stalled out and reversed, early morning rainfall totals were already elevated from Marshall/Calloway County to Todd County and further south in Tennessee. Heavy rains from afternoon supercells increased flooding issues, including washing out part of a road in Trigg County, while continued heavy rainfall late evening into the overnight hours left numerous roads flooded in Trigg County and in Hopkinsville. Rainfall totals were 5-8 inches in portions of Marshall and Trigg Counties and southernmost portions of Christian and Todd Counties. Both the Bypass and 68/80 gages on the South Fork of the Little River in Hopkinsville reached minor flood, and significant rainfall totals on the Green River resulted in Paradise reaching moderate flood stage a couple days later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A broad area of thunderstorm wind damage affected Calvert City. EM officials reported that trees were downed on Cedar Street. Spotters reported that four trees were snapped or uprooted on the north side of Calvert City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While a low pressure system spun over the Great Lakes region, active weather extended down into the Middle Ohio Valley and Central Appalachians. Disturbances embedded in this system provided support for showers and storms across the area. An initial round of sub-severe activity occurred with the passage of one such disturbance early on August 17th. Following a brief break, more robust convection began to develop in Ohio and Kentucky as a second upper disturbance moved overhead that afternoon. Broken lines of showers and storms progressed toward the Ohio River by mid-afternoon, downing a tree and power lines in Carter County before storms pushed east into West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blew down onto power lines along Hilton Branch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of July heat event continued through the afternoon on August 1st, with a line of storms crossing northwest Kentucky mid-afternoon, continuing through the Pennyrile late afternoon. Cooler and drier conditions remained afterwards. The highest heat index on the 1st in Western Kentucky was 120F at the Murray-Calloway County Regional Airport AWOS site.

A robust squall line sweeping through the I-64 corridor of Southern Illinois during the early afternoon hours on the 1st crossed into Northwestern Kentucky for mid-afternoon. Several trees were damaged in Henderson and Daviess Counties, with the storm line weakening as it moved through the Pennyrile. MLCAPE values were approximately 3000 J/kg, DCAPE around 1000 J/kg, and effective bulk shear was 30 kts. Scattered showers and storms continued through the evening and overnight as a cold front moved through during the night, with storm activity in the later convection generally remaining sub-severe aside from one cell which produced wind damage in McCracken County overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines were downed by thunderstorm winds near and along Carneal Rd, Noble Rd, and Bobo Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of Thursday August the 1st, a warm front was draped across portions of the state. The airmass was warm across the state, and storms were already firing off across portions of the state, including eastern Kentucky by 7am. However, the real action would come later in the day. A cold front located across Illinois, still well away from Kentucky, produced a line of storms along it during the morning hours. This line of storms gained strength, becoming a squall line and quickly dropping southeast. It reached central Kentucky by the early evening hours, then eastern Kentucky a couple hours later. By the time it got to eastern Kentucky, the line began to lose it's form, but embedded cells still packed a punch, resulting in numerous warnings across the CWA and just as many reports of wind damage. It began to regain it's linear shape by the time it reached the far eastern portion of the state, finally exiting just between 10 and 11pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clay county dispatch reported a tree blown down onto Keith Lane, near the community of Burning Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major outbreak of severe weather occurred on the 8th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a longwave trough was centered across the Rockies with deep-layer southwesterly flow from the Southern Plains to the Ohio Valley. A 110 kt upper jet extended from northern KS to IA while a 60 kt mid-level jet was positioned across northern MZ into IA. Surface low pressure near the MZ/KS border lifted to St. Louis around 12am CST on the 9th. A warm frontal boundary stretched across the Quad State, with a cold front extending southward from the surface low. ||The environment became unstable by early afternoon, especially in far Western Kentucky, with MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg. Deep-layer shear was on the order of 55-65 kts, though low-level shear was 15 kts, increasing to 20 kts later in the day. SRH was around 100-2000 m2/s2 with higher values north towards Clarksville, TN. STP values were 2-3 due to deep-layer shear and instability. Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon in Western Kentucky with lapse rates of 6.5-7 C/km aiding with hail production.||For Southwest Kentucky, a sub-severe line of storms moved through with a cold front during the overnight hours from the 7th-8th. The front reversed and lifted northward during the morning, producing thunderstorm wind damage in the Purchase area, particularly in and around Calvert City. Supercell development occurred during the afternoon hours with widespread hail and thunderstorm wind damage. An EF-U tornado was identified in eastern McCracken County. Several counties were hit multiple times over the course of the afternoon into the evening as lines of storms entered Northwest Kentucky from Southern Illinois. Daviess County experienced widespread significant wind damage in and west of Owensboro between 2030-2100CST, including 3 tornadoes (2 EF-0 and 1 EF-1). A powerful outflow boundary from the Daviess County storms pushed through Livermore producing a mile wide swath of significant wind damage. A final line of thunderstorms pushed through Western Kentucky late evening past midnight, with limited severe weather. ||Torrential rainfall produced flooding impacts across Western Kentucky. A northern high rain total swath extended from Union County to southern Daviess/McLean Counties with 3-5 inches of rain. Being the location where the cold front stalled out and reversed, early morning rainfall totals were already elevated from Marshall/Calloway County to Todd County and further south in Tennessee. Heavy rains from afternoon supercells increased flooding issues, including washing out part of a road in Trigg County, while continued heavy rainfall late evening into the overnight hours left numerous roads flooded in Trigg County and in Hopkinsville. Rainfall totals were 5-8 inches in portions of Marshall and Trigg Counties and southernmost portions of Christian and Todd Counties. Both the Bypass and 68/80 gages on the South Fork of the Little River in Hopkinsville reached minor flood, and significant rainfall totals on the Green River resulted in Paradise reaching moderate flood stage a couple days later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downburst winds from a supercell storm damaged a roof and destroyed an outhouse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major outbreak of severe weather occurred on the 8th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a longwave trough was centered across the Rockies with deep-layer southwesterly flow from the Southern Plains to the Ohio Valley. A 110 kt upper jet extended from northern KS to IA while a 60 kt mid-level jet was positioned across northern MZ into IA. Surface low pressure near the MZ/KS border lifted to St. Louis around 12am CST on the 9th. A warm frontal boundary stretched across the Quad State, with a cold front extending southward from the surface low. ||The environment became unstable by early afternoon, especially in far Western Kentucky, with MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg. Deep-layer shear was on the order of 55-65 kts, though low-level shear was 15 kts, increasing to 20 kts later in the day. SRH was around 100-2000 m2/s2 with higher values north towards Clarksville, TN. STP values were 2-3 due to deep-layer shear and instability. Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon in Western Kentucky with lapse rates of 6.5-7 C/km aiding with hail production.||For Southwest Kentucky, a sub-severe line of storms moved through with a cold front during the overnight hours from the 7th-8th. The front reversed and lifted northward during the morning, producing thunderstorm wind damage in the Purchase area, particularly in and around Calvert City. Supercell development occurred during the afternoon hours with widespread hail and thunderstorm wind damage. An EF-U tornado was identified in eastern McCracken County. Several counties were hit multiple times over the course of the afternoon into the evening as lines of storms entered Northwest Kentucky from Southern Illinois. Daviess County experienced widespread significant wind damage in and west of Owensboro between 2030-2100CST, including 3 tornadoes (2 EF-0 and 1 EF-1). A powerful outflow boundary from the Daviess County storms pushed through Livermore producing a mile wide swath of significant wind damage. A final line of thunderstorms pushed through Western Kentucky late evening past midnight, with limited severe weather. ||Torrential rainfall produced flooding impacts across Western Kentucky. A northern high rain total swath extended from Union County to southern Daviess/McLean Counties with 3-5 inches of rain. Being the location where the cold front stalled out and reversed, early morning rainfall totals were already elevated from Marshall/Calloway County to Todd County and further south in Tennessee. Heavy rains from afternoon supercells increased flooding issues, including washing out part of a road in Trigg County, while continued heavy rainfall late evening into the overnight hours left numerous roads flooded in Trigg County and in Hopkinsville. Rainfall totals were 5-8 inches in portions of Marshall and Trigg Counties and southernmost portions of Christian and Todd Counties. Both the Bypass and 68/80 gages on the South Fork of the Little River in Hopkinsville reached minor flood, and significant rainfall totals on the Green River resulted in Paradise reaching moderate flood stage a couple days later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported hail estimated at quarter size near U.S. 62 west of Paducah. Emergency management reported hail estimated at half dollar size near the intersection of New Holt Rd and Friendship Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system was in place across the Great Lakes region heading into the morning hours of Saturday, August 17. From this a cold front draped southwestward, moving towards the state throughout the day. By the time it reached western Kentucky during the afternoon hours. the warm/unstable airmass ahead of the boundary had already spawned showers and thunderstorms. A line of storms formed ahead of the front and continued to outpace the frontal passage eastward. By the time it reached eastern Kentucky, multiple severe thunderstorm warnings were issued for these strong storms. Throughout the afternoon and evening hours, widespread wind damage occurred in numerous counties. Multiple locations also saw damaging hail.  While the main line of storms fizzled out during the evening hours in the far east, additional discrete thunderstorms continued ahead and all the frontal boundary into the overnight for central and eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down and across Hwy-746 in Wellington. The exact location along Hwy-746 was not given.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of July heat event continued through the afternoon on August 1st, with a line of storms crossing northwest Kentucky mid-afternoon, continuing through the Pennyrile late afternoon. Cooler and drier conditions remained afterwards. The highest heat index on the 1st in Western Kentucky was 120F at the Murray-Calloway County Regional Airport AWOS site.

A robust squall line sweeping through the I-64 corridor of Southern Illinois during the early afternoon hours on the 1st crossed into Northwestern Kentucky for mid-afternoon. Several trees were damaged in Henderson and Daviess Counties, with the storm line weakening as it moved through the Pennyrile. MLCAPE values were approximately 3000 J/kg, DCAPE around 1000 J/kg, and effective bulk shear was 30 kts. Scattered showers and storms continued through the evening and overnight as a cold front moved through during the night, with storm activity in the later convection generally remaining sub-severe aside from one cell which produced wind damage in McCracken County overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were downed near Reed in far eastern Henderson County as a leading storm cell intensified with the arrival of an outflow boundary ahead of the main line of storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of Thursday August the 1st, a warm front was draped across portions of the state. The airmass was warm across the state, and storms were already firing off across portions of the state, including eastern Kentucky by 7am. However, the real action would come later in the day. A cold front located across Illinois, still well away from Kentucky, produced a line of storms along it during the morning hours. This line of storms gained strength, becoming a squall line and quickly dropping southeast. It reached central Kentucky by the early evening hours, then eastern Kentucky a couple hours later. By the time it got to eastern Kentucky, the line began to lose it's form, but embedded cells still packed a punch, resulting in numerous warnings across the CWA and just as many reports of wind damage. It began to regain it's linear shape by the time it reached the far eastern portion of the state, finally exiting just between 10 and 11pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Knox county dispatch reported trees blown down onto KY-225, near the intersection with Warren Camp Road, in the community of Warren.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into eastern Kentucky during the early afternoon hours on Wednesday, August 7th. It then became stationary for much of the remainder of the day, as Tropical Storm (downgraded from Hurricane) Debbie began to move along the southeast Atlantic Coast. This stationary boundary created the instability to produce showers and thunderstorms throughout the afternoon across the region, especially in the southwest coverage area along the actual boundary. However, with very slow flow aloft, it also led to some storms along the boundary developing and then becoming nearly stationary, dumping excessive rain over the same locations for an extended period of time. This ultimately led to some flooding in the Broadhead region of Rockcastle County, where some of the heaviest rains fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brodhead Emergency Management reported street flooding about 6 to 7 inches deep in Brodhead on West Main Street, South Main Street, and KY-1505 (Boone Way).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Main Street in Broadhead was down to one lane due to flood waters near 596 South Main Street, in the vicinity of the Broadhead Fairgrounds and Rockcastle City Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary set up along the Ohio River, allowing for afternoon convection to start popping along the boundary and just south in the warm sector. This included portions of northeast Kentucky. Several storms became strong, and ultimately three instances of wind damage were reported during the mid-afternoon hours. Storms then diminished heading into the evening hours with loss of afternoon heat and mixing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees were snapped at the base by thunderstorm wind gusts at consecutive properties along Cedar Creek Road in Pikeville. The locations along the road were not given.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure was drifting across the Quad State on the 14th. Showers and thunderstorms developed in the afternoon to evening with Western Kentucky seeing storms drift slowly, pivoting eastward to northeastward. CAPE values around 1000 J/kg dropped off after sunset. Lapse rates were around 6.5 C/km, and shear was limited to around 25 kts. A few of the storms became strong to severe with a tree falling onto a home near Eddyville and quarter size hail occurring on the south side of Princeton. Torrential rainfall of 3-5.5 inches fell in Henderson County, though flooding reports were confined to the western portions of the county. Flooding also occurred in Calloway County, with brief minor flooding in Christian County at the 68/80 Bypass site for the South Fork of the Little River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in Christian County from the May 14th storms resulted in rapid river level rises on the South Fork of the Little River with minor flooding occurring during the early evening hours of the 14th near the 68/80 site. Farmland flooding and some street flooding of nearby roads briefly occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second major severe weather outbreak for the month occurred on the 26th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a shortwave trough centered in the middle of the country with a 60 kt mid-level jet moved across northern Arkansas. A weak surface low was moving into SE Missouri during the morning with a secondary low located further northwest. A warm frontal boundary was draped across our area. A bowing line progressed east across SE Missouri into W Kentucky and far S Illinois between 0500-1000CST, followed by a mostly sub-severe second line that exited the region by 1300CST. The environment recovered producing fuel for more severe storms, starting with supercells 1800-2100CST with a line of storms moving east-southeastward across the entire Quad State after 1900CST, crossing through by midnight. Across the Quad State, this event produced the record for most tornado track miles in a single day in office history, along with the second most warnings issued in a day (just behind the May 8, 2024 event). Widespread straight-line wind and tornado damage resulted in over 100,000 customers without power across the Quad State.||For the morning, MLCAPE values at 0700CST were 1000-2000 J/kg in W Kentucky. Effective bulk shear was 30-40 kts with STP around 1, which increased in the following hours. Precipitable water was 1.5-1.8 inches, allowing for torrential rainfall rates.||The morning round of storms produced widespread destructive straight-line wind damage leading to numerous power outages across the southernmost portions of W Kentucky from the Jackson Purchase Area to the Southern Pennyrile. Two EF-1 tornadoes occurred in Calloway County, with one of them tracking 22 miles. One person was injured when thunderstorm winds caused a tree to fall on his vehicle on Rockwood Rd in Calloway County. Two more tornadoes formed in Trigg County, one an EF-1, and an EF-2 that produced its strongest damage south of Hopkinsville in Christian County. Another EF-1 tornado formed northwest of Bremen and tracked northeastward to Island in McLean County. Torrential rainfall rates led to a few reports of flash flooding in Western Kentucky during the morning to early afternoon hours. ||Heading into the evening, MLCAPE values at 1800CST were 2000-3500 J/kg in W Kentucky. For W Kentucky, effective bulk shear rose to 45-55 kts, with SRH around 200-400 m2/s2 and STP of 2-4. 0-1 km shear was 20-30 kts. Mid-level lapse rates were 7-7.5 C/km. DCAPE of 1100-1400 J/kg provided ample opportunity for downburst winds.||Late afternoon to early evening supercells produced hail reports and a long-track EF-3 tornado that began in Lyon County, then continued east-northeastward through Caldwell and Hopkins Counties. Severe structural damage occurred between Dawson Springs and Charleston and at least one family in Barnsley lost their home for the second time with the previous instance being the historic December 10, 2021 tornado. 21 people were injured (4 in Caldwell, 17 in Hopkins) while two people lost their lives, one in Hopkins County from the tornado (48f, direct), and one in Caldwell County due to a heart attack while cleaning up tornado debris (54m, indirect). The cold front swept through, producing more straight-line wind damage and flash flooding. As the line caught up to the supercell tornado, the storm became much more muddled, with an EF-1 tornado taking shape in Muhlenberg County tracking southeastward through Beech Creek and Beechmont, and continuing as a long track tornado reaching areas north of Bowling Green. Late night power outages were widespread along the tornado tracks in Lyon, Hopkins, Caldwell, and Muhlenberg Counties, which other counties with around half the county without power included Calloway, Trigg, Christian, and Todd Counties. Rainfall amounts of 2.5 to 4 inches were commonplace, with locally higher totals in southern Trigg and Christian Counties. Brief minor flooding occurred for the third time this month on part of the S. Fork Little River near Hopkinsville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in Christian County from the May 26th storms resulted in rapid river level rises on the South Fork of the Little River with minor flood stage reached just after midnight on the 27th at the 68/80 site with minimal impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second major severe weather outbreak for the month occurred on the 26th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a shortwave trough centered in the middle of the country with a 60 kt mid-level jet moved across northern Arkansas. A weak surface low was moving into SE Missouri during the morning with a secondary low located further northwest. A warm frontal boundary was draped across our area. A bowing line progressed east across SE Missouri into W Kentucky and far S Illinois between 0500-1000CST, followed by a mostly sub-severe second line that exited the region by 1300CST. The environment recovered producing fuel for more severe storms, starting with supercells 1800-2100CST with a line of storms moving east-southeastward across the entire Quad State after 1900CST, crossing through by midnight. Across the Quad State, this event produced the record for most tornado track miles in a single day in office history, along with the second most warnings issued in a day (just behind the May 8, 2024 event). EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system was in place across the Great Lakes region heading into the morning hours of Saturday, August 17. From this a cold front draped southwestward, moving towards the state throughout the day. By the time it reached western Kentucky during the afternoon hours. the warm/unstable airmass ahead of the boundary had already spawned showers and thunderstorms. A line of storms formed ahead of the front and continued to outpace the frontal passage eastward. By the time it reached eastern Kentucky, multiple severe thunderstorm warnings were issued for these strong storms. Throughout the afternoon and evening hours, widespread wind damage occurred in numerous counties. Multiple locations also saw damaging hail.  While the main line of storms fizzled out during the evening hours in the far east, additional discrete thunderstorms continued ahead and all the frontal boundary into the overnight for central and eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were downed along Chimney Top Road in the Red River Gorge. Exactly locations along the road were not given.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of July heat event continued through the afternoon on August 1st, with a line of storms crossing northwest Kentucky mid-afternoon, continuing through the Pennyrile late afternoon. Cooler and drier conditions remained afterwards. The highest heat index on the 1st in Western Kentucky was 120F at the Murray-Calloway County Regional Airport AWOS site.

On the morning of Thursday August the 1st, a warm front was draped across portions of the state. The airmass was warm across the state, and storms were already firing off across portions of the state, including eastern Kentucky by 7am. However, the real action would come later in the day. A cold front located across Illinois, still well away from Kentucky, produced a line of storms along it during the morning hours. This line of storms gained strength, becoming a squall line and quickly dropping southeast. It reached central Kentucky by the early evening hours, then eastern Kentucky a couple hours later. By the time it got to eastern Kentucky, the line began to lose it's form, but embedded cells still packed a punch, resulting in numerous warnings across the CWA and just as many reports of wind damage. It began to regain it's linear shape by the time it reached the far eastern portion of the state, finally exiting just between 10 and 11pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whitley county dispatch reported a tree blown down onto Raymond Ridge Road, near the community of Bee Creek and Youngs Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure was drifting across the Quad State on the 14th. Showers and thunderstorms developed in the afternoon to evening with Western Kentucky seeing storms drift slowly, pivoting eastward to northeastward. CAPE values around 1000 J/kg dropped off after sunset. Lapse rates were around 6.5 C/km, and shear was limited to around 25 kts. A few of the storms became strong to severe with a tree falling onto a home near Eddyville and quarter size hail occurring on the south side of Princeton. Torrential rainfall of 3-5.5 inches fell in Henderson County, though flooding reports were confined to the western portions of the county. Flooding also occurred in Calloway County, with brief minor flooding in Christian County at the 68/80 Bypass site for the South Fork of the Little River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were downed by thunderstorm winds, one falling onto a house in Eddyville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major outbreak of severe weather occurred on the 8th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a longwave trough was centered across the Rockies with deep-layer southwesterly flow from the Southern Plains to the Ohio Valley. A 110 kt upper jet extended from northern KS to IA while a 60 kt mid-level jet was positioned across northern MZ into IA. Surface low pressure near the MZ/KS border lifted to St. Louis around 12am CST on the 9th. A warm frontal boundary stretched across the Quad State, with a cold front extending southward from the surface low. ||The environment became unstable by early afternoon, especially in far Western Kentucky, with MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg. Deep-layer shear was on the order of 55-65 kts, though low-level shear was 15 kts, increasing to 20 kts later in the day. SRH was around 100-2000 m2/s2 with higher values north towards Clarksville, TN. STP values were 2-3 due to deep-layer shear and instability. Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon in Western Kentucky with lapse rates of 6.5-7 C/km aiding with hail production.||For Southwest Kentucky, a sub-severe line of storms moved through with a cold front during the overnight hours from the 7th-8th. The front reversed and lifted northward during the morning, producing thunderstorm wind damage in the Purchase area, particularly in and around Calvert City. Supercell development occurred during the afternoon hours with widespread hail and thunderstorm wind damage. An EF-U tornado was identified in eastern McCracken County. Several counties were hit multiple times over the course of the afternoon into the evening as lines of storms entered Northwest Kentucky from Southern Illinois. Daviess County experienced widespread significant wind damage in and west of Owensboro between 2030-2100CST, including 3 tornadoes (2 EF-0 and 1 EF-1). A powerful outflow boundary from the Daviess County storms pushed through Livermore producing a mile wide swath of significant wind damage. A final line of thunderstorms pushed through Western Kentucky late evening past midnight, with limited severe weather. ||Torrential rainfall produced flooding impacts across Western Kentucky. A northern high rain total swath extended from Union County to southern Daviess/McLean Counties with 3-5 inches of rain. Being the location where the cold front stalled out and reversed, early morning rainfall totals were already elevated from Marshall/Calloway County to Todd County and further south in Tennessee. Heavy rains from afternoon supercells increased flooding issues, including washing out part of a road in Trigg County, while continued heavy rainfall late evening into the overnight hours left numerous roads flooded in Trigg County and in Hopkinsville. Rainfall totals were 5-8 inches in portions of Marshall and Trigg Counties and southernmost portions of Christian and Todd Counties. Both the Bypass and 68/80 gages on the South Fork of the Little River in Hopkinsville reached minor flood, and significant rainfall totals on the Green River resulted in Paradise reaching moderate flood stage a couple days later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measured to quarter size was reported in Hopkinsville due to a thunderstorm line moving through just after midnight.

On the morning of Thursday August the 1st, a warm front was draped across portions of the state. The airmass was warm across the state, and storms were already firing off across portions of the state, including eastern Kentucky by 7am. However, the real action would come later in the day. A cold front located across Illinois, still well away from Kentucky, produced a line of storms along it during the morning hours. This line of storms gained strength, becoming a squall line and quickly dropping southeast. It reached central Kentucky by the early evening hours, then eastern Kentucky a couple hours later. By the time it got to eastern Kentucky, the line began to lose it's form, but embedded cells still packed a punch, resulting in numerous warnings across the CWA and just as many reports of wind damage. It began to regain it's linear shape by the time it reached the far eastern portion of the state, finally exiting just between 10 and 11pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down on road to Jonesville Boat Ramp, south of the community of Keno.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major outbreak of severe weather occurred on the 8th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a longwave trough was centered across the Rockies with deep-layer southwesterly flow from the Southern Plains to the Ohio Valley. A 110 kt upper jet extended from northern KS to IA while a 60 kt mid-level jet was positioned across northern MZ into IA. Surface low pressure near the MZ/KS border lifted to St. Louis around 12am CST on the 9th. A warm frontal boundary stretched across the Quad State, with a cold front extending southward from the surface low. ||The environment became unstable by early afternoon, especially in far Western Kentucky, with MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg. Deep-layer shear was on the order of 55-65 kts, though low-level shear was 15 kts, increasing to 20 kts later in the day. SRH was around 100-2000 m2/s2 with higher values north towards Clarksville, TN. STP values were 2-3 due to deep-layer shear and instability. Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon in Western Kentucky with lapse rates of 6.5-7 C/km aiding with hail production.||For Southwest Kentucky, a sub-severe line of storms moved through with a cold front during the overnight hours from the 7th-8th. The front reversed and lifted northward during the morning, producing thunderstorm wind damage in the Purchase area, particularly in and around Calvert City. Supercell development occurred during the afternoon hours with widespread hail and thunderstorm wind damage. An EF-U tornado was identified in eastern McCracken County. Several counties were hit multiple times over the course of the afternoon into the evening as lines of storms entered Northwest Kentucky from Southern Illinois. Daviess County experienced widespread significant wind damage in and west of Owensboro between 2030-2100CST, including 3 tornadoes (2 EF-0 and 1 EF-1). A powerful outflow boundary from the Daviess County storms pushed through Livermore producing a mile wide swath of significant wind damage. A final line of thunderstorms pushed through Western Kentucky late evening past midnight, with limited severe weather. ||Torrential rainfall produced flooding impacts across Western Kentucky. A northern high rain total swath extended from Union County to southern Daviess/McLean Counties with 3-5 inches of rain. Being the location where the cold front stalled out and reversed, early morning rainfall totals were already elevated from Marshall/Calloway County to Todd County and further south in Tennessee. Heavy rains from afternoon supercells increased flooding issues, including washing out part of a road in Trigg County, while continued heavy rainfall late evening into the overnight hours left numerous roads flooded in Trigg County and in Hopkinsville. Rainfall totals were 5-8 inches in portions of Marshall and Trigg Counties and southernmost portions of Christian and Todd Counties. Both the Bypass and 68/80 gages on the South Fork of the Little River in Hopkinsville reached minor flood, and significant rainfall totals on the Green River resulted in Paradise reaching moderate flood stage a couple days later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down briefly just north of Rome, KY. One farm outbuilding was severely damaged, with heavy machinery tossed or pushed into nearby open fields. A grain bin and outbuildings were also damaged. Two large trees were snapped at the base, and several large limbs were downed. The estimated peak wind was 95 mph. The path length was under one mile and the maximum path width was 100 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet accompanying a passing disturbance led to thousands of customers without power across eastern Kentucky on December 29th due to tree and power pole damage. While some isolated wind gusts accompanied late morning strong thunderstorms with a cold front, gusts picked up in earnest later that afternoon and evening as the jet winds aloft were able to mix down to the surface. In Kentucky, the strongest wind observation occurred at Mesonet station in Boyd County of 55 miles per hour on the evening of the 29th. Generally around the area, gusts up to 40 to 45 miles per hour were common in the wake of the frontal passage, contributing to widespread power outages late that afternoon into the evening hours. While still breezy overnight, the low level jet shifted northward, eliminating the stronger wind gusts heading into December 30th.

On New Years Eve 2024 high winds were in place across eastern Kentucky as a strong low pressure system neared the state from the west, becoming occluded as it passing just to the north of the Ohio River. Strong SW flow took hold as a result, before the associated cold front moved through during the late morning/early afternoon hours. This system was connected to a strong upper level low pressure system as well, which resulted in tight pressure gradients across the region. With winds able to mix down during the day, some of these higher winds aloft made it down to the surface, especially ahead of the cold front. A wind advisory was issued across much of the region as a result. Many measuring sites saw wind gusts above 40mph throughout the morning hours, but the Wayne County Mesonet was the only one to reach 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wayne County Mesonet station, located 3 miles WNW of Mill Springs, recorded a non-thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph at 8am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At 0Z on Sunday December 29th, a low pressure was in place across the Lower Mississippi Valley, moving northeastward, reaching southern Indiana by 15Z. As it moved, it strengthened considerably. It lifted a warm front through the state by 6Z, as strong S to SW winds began to flow across eastern Kentucky. The cold front then followed, passing through eastern Kentucky during the late morning and early afternoon hours. This system was also accompanied by a very steep trough, which also increased the winds aloft given the tight pressure gradients. Some of these winds were able to mix down to the surface, especially ahead of and along the cold frontal passage as it moved through. This prompted the midnight shift to issue a wind advisory for much of eastern Kentucky during the overnight hours leading into the event. Ultimately, quite a few counties saw wind gusts above 40 mph, however Rowan County clocked the highest speed, at 58 mph in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rowan County Kentucky Mesonet station, located 2 miles SW of Sharkey, measured a non-thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph at 4:15pm EST on Sunday 12/29/2024. Less than an hour later, at 5:02pm EST, another Kentucky Mesonet station in Rowan County, this one located atop Triangle Mountain, clocked a 55 mph wind gust. Mutliple other counties, including Pulsaski, Harlan, and Morgan counties, saw wind gusts of 50 to 51 mph during the afternoon, with even more clocking above 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across the Ohio and Tennessee valleys on May 26th and into the early morning hours of May 27th, 2024. Two waves of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across central Kentucky, producing numerous occurrences of straight-line wind damage and 8 tornadoes while crossing the region. The first wave of storms moved across central Kentucky as a large bowing quasi-linear convective system during the late morning and early afternoon hours. This line of storms produced widespread straight-line wind damage, with particularly heavy damage observed across the Louisville metro and northern Woodford County. Two quick spin up tornadoes in Clinton County were also associated with this line.||After a second sub-severe line of storms moved across the area during the early afternoon hours, conditions were relatively quiet until the mid-to-late afternoon, when strong and severe cells began to develop across western Kentucky and southern Illinois. The storms would initially be discrete before growing into another large line of storms which produced the heaviest damage south of Interstate 64. Six tornadoes would be associated with this second line, with a long track EF1 tornado extending for over 40 miles across Muhlenberg, Butler, and Warren County. Heavy straight-line wind damage was also observed with this second line of storms, especially in Barren, Mercer, and Garrard County. The line of storms finally pushed east of the area shortly after midnight on May 27th, bringing an end to an active 16 hours across central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service conducted a storm survey and found a|tornado occurred on Sunday, May 26th, 2024. Despite the fact that the tornado was only on the ground for a little over one mile and one minute, it tracked very briefly through Monroe, Metcalfe, and Cumberland County. Starting in Monroe|County near the Bacon Branch of Leatherwood Creek on a rural|hillside, drone footage found a clearly defined path of uprooted|trees with a convergent signature to the damage consistent with|100 mph winds. The tornado then turned along the creek and|crossed into the intersection of Cumberland County and Metcalfe|County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service conducted a storm survey and found a tornado occurred on Sunday, May 26th, 2024. Despite the fact that the tornado was only on the ground for a little over one mile and one minute, it tracked very briefly through Monroe, Metcalfe, and Cumberland County.||The tornado crossed into Metcalfe County for a few yards as it tracked along Bacon Branch of Leatherwood Creek near Fowler Brown Road. Most of the tree damage associated with the tornado at this point was in Cumberland County, though minor damage did occur across the county line in Metcalfe County. After a few seconds straddling the Metcalfe and Cumberland County line, the tornado would cross into Cumberland County for good.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak clipper system caused a brief period of light snow during the afternoon hours on the 2nd. Temperatures had initially been around 32 degrees, but dropped into the 20s later that day and evening. While snow accumulations between a coating to one inch were confined to mainly grassy and elevated surfaces, slick road conditions eventually developed in time for the evening commute that caused accidents across the region. A deadly three-vehicle collision had occurred on Interstate-24 near mile point 40 in Lyon County, KY. Two people were killed as a result of the crash that led to traffic being diverted for approximately 7 hours. Ice covered bridges and areas of black ice continued through the evening hours after snow had ended. Key Impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents.

A 996mb surface low pressure center near St Louis Missouri in the early morning hours of the 31st shifted east through the day. As a result, strong southwest winds in the predawn hours produced a brief gust up to 42 mph at the Madisonville AWOS station (K2I0) around 5 AM CST. As the low moved east, winds shifted to the west northwest and gusted 40 to 42 mph around noon from Marion to Henderson to Owensboro.

As a strong storm system approached, two lines of thunderstorms moved eastward through west Kentucky late on the 17th and early on the 18th. Torrential rainfall from the second line of storms produced some street flooding in Madisonville around 4 AM CST on the 18th. Previously, that line of storms produced estimated wind gusts up to 55 mph in Mayfield at 312 AM CST. The two rounds of thunderstorms, combined with heavy rainfall from the previous few days, helped to produce minor flooding of agricultural areas along the Green River near Paradise. The flooding began in the evening of the 18th and came to an end on the afternoon of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding flooding of low-lying agricultural areas occurred along the Green River at near Paradise. The river rose to a crest of 383.62 feet at 145 AM CST on the 21st.

As the low moved east, winds shifted to the west northwest and gusted 40 to 42 mph around noon from Marion to Henderson to Owensboro.

As a strong storm system approached, two lines of thunderstorms moved eastward through west Kentucky late on the 17th and early on the 18th. Torrential rainfall from the second line of storms produced some street flooding in Madisonville around 4 AM CST on the 18th. Previously, that line of storms produced estimated wind gusts up to 55 mph in Mayfield at 312 AM CST. The two rounds of thunderstorms, combined with heavy rainfall from the previous few days, helped to produce minor flooding of agricultural areas along the Green River near Paradise. The flooding began in the evening of the 18th and came to an end on the afternoon of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on McCoy Street, as brief torrential rainfall occurred with a fast-moving line of thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust dry cold frontal passage brought gusty post-frontal winds through the Evansville Tri-State area overnight on the 4th into the 5th. Winds gusted to 54mph at Owensboro-Daviess County Airport (KOWB) and 44mph at the KY Mesonet site northwest of Calhoun in McLean County. Gusts of 38-39mph were also observed in Union and Henderson Counties.

A rapidly developing low pressure system moved northeastward through the Quad State region from the evening of the 28th through the morning of the 29th. Standing water occurred in Fulton and Lyon Counties during the evening as the first round of rain moved through. Strong northwesterly wind gusts were observed in Northwest Kentucky late morning on the 29th as a wrap-around band of showers moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observation was measured at Mesonet station PVRT, located 5 miles northwest of Calhoun.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening hours of December 4th, a cold front progressed through the Ohio Valley and promoted strong to gusty winds in its wake. The front arrived into eastern Kentucky around midnight and swept through the area during the early morning on the 5th. The strongest wind gust for the area was measured in Boyd County, where a Mesonet station observed a 49 mile per hour gust near the town of Ashland. Gusts of 35 to 45 miles per hour were common around the state during that time as the front continued its passage overhead. Downed trees and power lines resulted in thousands of power outages to customers within eastern Kentucky. Winds subsided quickly after sunrise on the 5th as high pressure built into the Tennessee Valley.
